proposals.title: A Multi-Objective Optimization Framework for Building Energy Management Integrating Infection Risk and VOC Exposure Metrics
proposals.motivation: Building energy management systems (BEMS) traditionally prioritize energy efficiency, often leading to reduced ventilation and potential degradation of indoor air quality (IAQ). This creates a critical trade-off, as poor IAQ increases exposure to airborne infectious agents and volatile organic compounds (VOCs), with significant public health implications. Existing energy simulation tools lack integrated, quantitative health outcome metrics to guide optimization, a gap highlighted by the post-pandemic focus on infection-resilient buildings and ongoing concerns over indoor chemical pollution.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating dynamic, physics-based models of airborne infection risk (e.g., Wells-Riley) and VOC concentration into a whole-building energy simulation engine, a multi-objective optimization algorithm can identify operational strategies that effectively balance energy use with quantifiable health risk reduction.
proposals.method: The research will adapt and extend the EnergyPlus building energy model by coupling it with contaminant transport modules. Key health metrics—infection risk probability for a reference pathogen and cumulative exposure to a target VOC mixture—will be calculated in real-time within the simulation. A Non-dominated Sorting Genetic Algorithm (NSGA-II) will be implemented to perform multi-objective optimization, treating energy use, infection risk, and VOC exposure as competing objectives. The framework will be tested on a calibrated model of a public building under varying occupancy and seasonal scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, open-source simulation-optimization workflow that explicitly quantifies health-IAQ trade-offs in building operation. It will provide a methodological advance for holistic building performance assessment, moving beyond energy-centric metrics. The resulting Pareto-optimal solutions will offer actionable guidance for designing intelligent control systems that mitigate health risks without excessive energy penalty.
proposals.novelty: While energy modeling and IAQ assessment exist separately, their tight integration into a single automated optimization loop for explicit health outcome minimization is novel. This approach moves beyond reviewing technologies (e.g., TiO2) or assessing single risks, instead providing a systems-level decision-support tool that directly addresses the conflicting objectives inherent in sustainable building operation.

proposals.title: A Hybrid Supervised-Unsupervised Learning Framework for Online Fault Detection and Diagnosis in Building Ventilation Systems
proposals.motivation: The reliable operation of building ventilation systems is critical for indoor air quality (IAQ), energy efficiency, and infection risk mitigation, as highlighted by recent research. Existing Fault Detection and Diagnosis (FDD) methods often struggle with the dynamic, multi-fault nature of real systems; purely supervised methods require extensive labeled fault data, which is scarce, while purely unsupervised methods may lack diagnostic specificity. A robust online FDD method is needed to ensure systems operate as designed to control VOCs, pollutants, and airborne pathogens.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hybrid learning framework, which synergistically combines the anomaly detection capability of unsupervised learning with the diagnostic precision of supervised learning, will achieve higher accuracy and robustness in online FDD for ventilation systems compared to using either approach in isolation.
proposals.method: The method will develop a two-stage online hybrid model. First, an unsupervised Variational Autoencoder (VAE) will continuously model normal operational data (e.g., airflow, pressure, VOC concentrations) to detect anomalies and novel faults. Second, a supervised Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) will diagnose known fault types using labeled historical data. A novel online adaptation module will use the VAE's reconstruction error to trigger retraining or confidence calibration of the CNN when novel fault patterns emerge, creating a self-improving loop. Validation will use co-simulation (EnergyPlus/TRNSYS with a Python agent) and data from real building management systems.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a validated, adaptable FDD framework specifically tailored for the dynamic fault profiles of ventilation and IAQ control systems. It will provide a practical pathway to implement intelligent operation and maintenance, directly supporting energy efficiency and infection risk mitigation goals. The methodology will be disseminated as an open-source tool for building control practitioners.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the specific integration mechanism for online adaptation, where the unsupervised model actively guides the supervised model's evolution in real-time, moving beyond simple ensemble or sequential hybrid approaches common in pre-2026 literature. This addresses the critical challenge of diagnosing both known and novel faults in continuously operating systems.

proposals.title: A Robust Data Preprocessing Framework for Building Automation Systems: Enabling Reliable Infection Risk and VOC Exposure Assessment
proposals.motivation: Building Automation System (BAS) data is critical for intelligent control of ventilation to mitigate airborne infection risk and manage indoor VOC concentrations. However, real-world BAS data is plagued by noise, missing values, and unlabeled or misconfigured sensor points, which severely undermines the reliability of data-driven models for health risk assessment and energy efficiency. Without robust preprocessing, the performance of advanced control systems, including those for photocatalytic oxidation integration, remains suboptimal and unpredictable.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hybrid preprocessing framework combining physics-informed data validation with self-supervised learning for signal reconstruction can significantly improve the quality and usability of raw BAS data streams. This will, in turn, enhance the accuracy of downstream models predicting indoor contaminant concentrations and infection risk.
proposals.method: The method will integrate: 1) A rule-based validation module using pre-2026 knowledge of HVAC system physics and mass balance constraints to flag implausible readings. 2) A deep learning-based imputation and denoising pipeline using a pre-trained Temporal Convolutional Network (TCN) or Transformer architecture, trained on high-fidelity building simulation data (e.g., EnergyPlus) and fine-tuned in a self-supervised manner on real BAS data. 3) A clustering-based approach (e.g., variational autoencoders) for inferring labels for unclassified sensor points by analyzing temporal patterns relative to known system operations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a standardized, open-source preprocessing toolkit specifically designed for the unique challenges of BAS data, moving beyond generic imputation methods. It will directly enable more accurate real-time calculation of infection risk (e.g., using Wells-Riley or dose-response models) and VOC exposure indices, forming a reliable foundation for the intelligent control systems central to the researcher's recent work.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tailored fusion of domain-specific HVAC physical constraints with modern self-supervised deep learning for temporal data, creating a preprocessing framework that is both interpretable and powerful for the heterogeneous, weakly-labeled data streams typical of BAS, which is not addressed by general time-series methods.

proposals.title: A Graph Neural Network Framework for Multi-Fault Detection and Diagnosis in Complex HVAC Systems by Transferring Social Network Embedding Techniques
proposals.motivation: Modern HVAC systems are complex networks of spatially and functionally interdependent components. Traditional fault detection and diagnosis (FDD) methods often treat sensors and components in isolation, failing to capture these critical relationships, which leads to missed or misdiagnosed faults. This reduces system reliability, increases energy consumption, and can compromise indoor air quality and infection risk control, which are central concerns in building management.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling an HVAC system as a heterogeneous graph of components (e.g., chillers, ducts, dampers, sensors) and applying transfer learning principles from social network Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) will significantly improve the accuracy and generalizability of multi-fault diagnosis by explicitly learning the propagation of fault signatures through the system's topology.
proposals.method: We will construct a heterogeneous graph where nodes represent physical components and sensors, and edges encode spatial adjacency, airflow paths, and control signal dependencies. Inspired by relational GNNs used for social networks (e.g., RGCN, GraphSAGE), we will adapt these architectures to learn embeddings for HVAC components. The model will be trained on simulated fault data (e.g., from EnergyPlus or Modelica) for common faults like stuck dampers, sensor drift, and coil fouling. A key technical step is the transfer and adaptation of the attention or message-passing mechanisms from social link prediction to modeling fault propagation dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, graph-based FDD framework that explicitly models system interdependencies, moving beyond point-based anomaly detection. It will provide a more robust and interpretable tool for building operators to maintain system health, thereby supporting energy efficiency and consistent indoor environmental quality. The successful transfer of social network GNN concepts will also demonstrate a new cross-domain application for these algorithms.
proposals.novelty: While GNNs are established in social and citation networks, their application to HVAC FDD is nascent. The novelty lies in the specific translation of relational modeling techniques from social systems (where edges model human interactions) to physical systems where edges represent engineered thermodynamic and control relationships, creating a new paradigm for systemic fault diagnosis.

proposals.title: A Graph-Based Fault Detection and Diagnosis Framework for District Energy Networks: Integrating Energy Efficiency and Indoor Air Quality Risk Metrics
proposals.motivation: District energy systems and interconnected building HVAC clusters are critical for urban decarbonization, but their complexity makes fault detection and diagnosis (FDD) challenging. Current FDD methods are largely confined to single buildings, ignoring cross-system interactions that can propagate faults, degrade energy performance, and compromise indoor air quality (IAQ) across multiple buildings, posing a systemic risk for infection transmission and VOC exposure.
proposals.hypothesis: A graph-based FDD framework that models the district energy network as an interconnected system can more accurately detect, diagnose, and prioritize faults by simultaneously analyzing energy anomalies and their propagated impact on IAQ risk metrics across the network.
proposals.method: The method will construct a graph model of the district network, with nodes representing buildings or subsystems (e.g., air handling units) and edges representing thermal/fluid connections and occupant flow. We will extend single-building FDD algorithms (e.g., PCA, rule-based methods) to this graph using Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) to capture spatial dependencies. Fault signatures will be defined using multi-objective metrics combining energy deviation (kWh) and IAQ risk indices (e.g., rebreathed fraction for infection, VOC concentration). A two-stage diagnosis will first localize the fault to a subgraph, then identify the specific component and fault type.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first FDD framework specifically designed for the multi-scale, multi-objective nature of district energy systems. It will deliver a scalable tool for operators to prioritize faults that have the greatest combined impact on energy waste and public health risk, moving beyond single-building optimization.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integrated graph-based modeling of physical energy flows and IAQ risk propagation for FDD, a significant departure from existing methods that treat buildings in isolation or focus solely on energy signals.

proposals.title: A Hybrid Adaptive-Personalized Thermal Comfort Model for Intelligent Building Operation: Integrating Occupancy Patterns, Real-Time Feedback, and Infection Risk Mitigation
proposals.motivation: Static thermal comfort models, like PMV-PPD, are widely used in building HVAC control but often fail to account for dynamic occupancy and individual variability, leading to energy waste and occupant dissatisfaction. The COVID-19 pandemic has further highlighted the need for ventilation strategies that balance infection risk mitigation with energy efficiency, a challenge poorly addressed by fixed-setpoint systems. An adaptive, data-driven approach can optimize this trade-off in real-time.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating adaptive thermal comfort standards (like ASHRAE 55's adaptive model) with personalized feedback and real-time occupancy sensing will yield a dynamic control model that significantly reduces predicted energy use for HVAC while maintaining or improving occupant satisfaction and managing airborne infection risk.
proposals.method: The research will develop a multi-agent simulation framework for a public building zone. The core algorithm will be a model predictive control (MPC) system. Inputs will include: 1) real-time occupancy density (from simulated CO2/PIR sensors), 2) a personalized comfort feedback loop via a simulated mobile app, and 3) a dynamic infection risk model based on Wells-Riley equations. The MPC will optimize setpoints using a cost function weighting energy use (predicted via a building energy model) against comfort deviation and infection probability. The adaptive comfort temperature will be the baseline, algorithmically adjusted by personalized offsets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated simulation framework for next-generation HVAC control that explicitly quantifies the trade-offs between energy, personalized comfort, and health risk. It will advance the field of intelligent building operation by moving beyond static setpoints to a responsive, multi-objective control paradigm. The results will offer practical guidance for integrating occupant-centric data streams into building management systems.
proposals.novelty: While adaptive comfort and personalized models exist separately, their integration with a dynamic infection risk model within a real-time MPC framework for operational control is novel. This approach directly addresses the post-pandemic need to unify health, comfort, and efficiency objectives in a single, automated control logic, a gap identified in recent reviews of intelligent building systems.

proposals.title: A Health-First Model Predictive Control Framework for Building Ventilation: Minimizing Infection and VOC Exposure Under Dynamic Energy Constraints
proposals.motivation: Post-pandemic, there is a critical need to re-evaluate building control paradigms that traditionally prioritize energy efficiency, often at the expense of indoor air quality (IAQ) and occupant health. Pre-2026 research by Haghighat and others highlights the significant risks from airborne infections and volatile organic compounds (VOCs), yet integrated control strategies that explicitly treat health outcomes as the primary objective, with energy as a constraint, are lacking. This shift is essential for designing resilient, healthy buildings that align with emerging public health and sustainability goals.
proposals.hypothesis: A model predictive control (MPC) framework that inverts the optimization priority—minimizing a composite health risk index for infection and VOC exposure subject to a dynamic energy budget constraint—will achieve superior IAQ outcomes compared to energy-first MPC, without a proportional increase in net energy consumption when integrated with real-time grid carbon intensity signals.
proposals.method: The framework will integrate: 1) a physics-based building energy and contaminant transport model, 2) a health risk module quantifying infection probability (via Wells-Riley or dose-response) and VOC exposure (using concentration- and species-dependent toxicity indices), and 3) a multi-objective MPC optimizer. The core algorithm will solve for optimal ventilation rates and air cleaner operation (inspired by TiO2 photocatalytic review) to minimize the health risk index, subject to a time-varying energy constraint derived from real-time electricity carbon intensity data and peak demand limits. Testing will use co-simulation of EnergyPlus and CONTAM under different climate and occupancy scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantifiable control methodology that formally embeds occupant health as the primary control objective in building automation. It will deliver a scalable MPC framework and demonstrate, through simulation, the trade-off curves between health risk mitigation and energy use under dynamic constraints, offering a practical pathway for building codes and standards to adopt health-centric performance metrics.
proposals.novelty: While MPC for buildings exists, inverting the core objective from energy cost to a multi-pathogen, multi-contaminant health risk index is a fundamental paradigm shift. The novelty lies in the explicit formulation of energy as a dynamic constraint rather than the primary cost function, and the integration of real-time grid carbon data to align health prioritization with broader carbon neutrality goals.

proposals.title: A Multi-Objective Model Predictive Control Framework for HVAC Systems Balancing Infection Risk, VOC Exposure, and Energy Use
proposals.motivation: Building HVAC systems face the conflicting demands of energy efficiency and occupant health protection. Post-pandemic, mitigating airborne infection risk is a critical IAQ parameter, while VOC exposure remains a persistent concern. Current control strategies often treat these objectives separately or use static rules, lacking a dynamic, integrated framework to navigate real-time trade-offs.
proposals.hypothesis: A model predictive control (MPC) framework that dynamically weights a multi-objective cost function—incorporating predicted infection risk (via Wells-Riley or dose-response models), VOC concentration, thermal comfort (PMV/PPD), and energy consumption—can achieve superior Pareto-optimal solutions compared to conventional setpoint-based or single-objective optimal controls.
proposals.method: We will develop a grey-box building energy and indoor air quality model for simulation. The core is an MPC with a cost function: J = w1*E + w2*(1-θ) + w3*[VOC] + w4*PMV^2, where θ is the infection probability. Weights (w_i) will be dynamically adjusted using a fuzzy logic supervisor responding to real-time occupancy density and outdoor conditions. The MPC will optimize setpoints for ventilation rate, air recirculation ratio (with consideration for photocatalytic oxidation integration), and temperature. Algorithms will be tested in a simulated office environment using pre-2026 tools like TRNSYS/Contam co-simulation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantifiable control methodology that explicitly manages the trade-off between infection risk mitigation and energy use, extending beyond traditional comfort-energy paradigms. It will deliver a scalable control architecture and actionable insights into dynamic priority-setting for healthy building operations.
proposals.novelty: The explicit, dynamic integration of a time-varying infection risk model with VOC and thermal comfort indices within a single MPC optimization loop is novel. The use of a fuzzy logic-based weight adaptation mechanism responsive to occupancy and external air quality to manage this multi-objective problem represents a significant advance over static weighting schemes.

proposals.title: A Robust Model Predictive Control Framework for Demand-Controlled Ventilation Integrating Infection Risk and Energy Use Under Occupancy Prediction Uncertainty
proposals.motivation: Demand-controlled ventilation (DCV) is critical for balancing indoor air quality (IAQ), infection risk mitigation, and energy efficiency in public buildings. However, real-world occupancy prediction models are inherently uncertain, leading to suboptimal or even risky control decisions when conventional deterministic controllers fail to account for this uncertainty. This gap limits the reliable deployment of intelligent building control systems for simultaneous health protection and carbon neutrality goals.
proposals.hypothesis: A robust Model Predictive Control (MPC) framework that explicitly incorporates quantified uncertainty in occupancy predictions will maintain acceptable bounds for infection risk and VOC concentrations while using less energy than conservative, static ventilation strategies or naive predictive controllers.
proposals.method: The research will develop a stochastic MPC architecture using Gaussian Process models to generate not only occupancy predictions but also associated uncertainty quantifications. These will serve as time-varying constraints within the MPC optimization problem. The cost function will minimize energy use, weighted against the probabilistic violation of constraints for key IAQ indicators: a rebreathed fraction-based infection risk metric and total VOC concentration. The controller's robustness will be tested in simulation using the TRNSYS/Contam co-simulation platform under a range of erroneous occupancy prediction scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel control design methodology that makes DCV systems resilient to sensor and prediction errors. It will deliver a quantifiable trade-off analysis between energy savings and guaranteed IAQ/health risk levels under uncertainty, directly contributing to the design of next-generation intelligent building operation systems.
proposals.novelty: While MPC for buildings exists, the explicit integration of stochastic occupancy prediction with dual health-risk constraints (infection and VOCs) in a single robust control formulation is novel. This moves beyond typical temperature/energy optimization or deterministic IAQ control, addressing a core reliability challenge in post-pandemic, health-aware building automation.

proposals.title: Model-Free Reinforcement Learning for Multi-Objective HVAC Control: Minimizing Infection Risk and VOC Exposure Under Energy Constraints
proposals.motivation: Building HVAC systems face the complex challenge of simultaneously managing energy use, indoor air quality (IAQ), and airborne infection risk. Traditional rule-based or model-predictive control often struggles with the dynamic, high-dimensional nature of these competing objectives, especially given the stochastic occupancy and pollutant generation (e.g., VOCs, bioaerosols). A data-driven approach that can learn optimal trade-offs in real-time is critically needed for resilient and healthy buildings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model-free deep reinforcement learning (DRL) agent, trained through direct interaction with a building environment, can discover non-obvious control policies that outperform conventional methods by dynamically balancing infection risk mitigation (via ventilation), VOC concentration reduction, and energy efficiency.
proposals.method: We will develop a soft actor-critic (SAC) DRL agent to control HVAC setpoints (e.g., airflow rates, recirculation ratios). The agent's reward function will integrate a weighted sum of normalized costs: energy consumption, a Wells-Riley-based infection risk metric, and TVOC concentration. Training will occur in a high-fidelity building performance simulation environment (e.g., EnergyPlus + CONTAM co-simulation) to generate interaction data. The state space will include indoor/outdoor conditions, real-time occupancy, and pollutant concentrations, while actions will modulate ventilation and filtration systems.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel DRL framework specifically tailored for multi-objective IAQ and health-risk management, moving beyond pure energy-focused control. It will provide a proof-of-concept for 'self-optimizing' HVAC systems that adapt to real-time health threats. The resulting control policies and their trade-off analysis will offer new insights for designing next-generation building operation systems.
proposals.novelty: While RL has been explored for building energy control, its application to directly minimize dynamic infection risk and VOC exposure using a model-free, robotics-inspired approach is novel. This integrates Dr. Haghighat's expertise in VOC/photocatalytic systems and infection risk modeling into a cutting-edge, adaptive control paradigm without requiring an explicit, perfect dynamical model of the building.

proposals.title: A Federated Transfer Learning Framework for Privacy-Preserving, Cross-Building Occupancy Modeling to Optimize Ventilation and Infection Risk Control
proposals.motivation: Effective demand-controlled ventilation (DCV) relies on accurate occupancy estimation to balance indoor air quality (IAQ), infection risk mitigation, and energy efficiency. However, training robust, generalizable occupancy models is hindered by the privacy-sensitive nature of detailed sensor data from individual buildings and the unique operational characteristics of each site. Pre-2026, there is a critical need for a method that leverages diverse building data without compromising data privacy or requiring centralized data aggregation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a federated learning framework, enhanced with transfer learning techniques, can collaboratively train a high-performance occupancy prediction model across multiple heterogeneous buildings, preserving data privacy while achieving accuracy comparable to a model trained on centralized data.
proposals.method: We will develop a cross-silo federated learning architecture where each building (client) trains a local Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) model on its private time-series data (e.g., CO2, VOC, motion sensor readings). The central server will aggregate model parameters using the Federated Averaging (FedAvg) algorithm. To address data heterogeneity (non-IID data), we will integrate a transfer learning component, specifically a lightweight domain adaptation layer, to fine-tune the global federated model for each building's specific conditions. The final model will output real-time occupancy estimates to drive a model predictive control (MPC) strategy for ventilation systems.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, privacy-by-design methodology for developing data-driven building control models. It will advance the field of intelligent building operation by demonstrating how federated learning can overcome data silos for IAQ and infection risk management. The resulting framework will be a key enabler for scalable, ethical smart building analytics.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of transfer learning principles directly into a federated learning workflow for building occupancy modeling, specifically targeting the challenge of data distribution shift across different ventilation systems and usage patterns, a gap not addressed in pre-2026 federated learning or standalone DCV research.

proposals.title: A Risk-Based Multi-Objective Framework for Optimizing IAQ Control Strategies: Challenging the Primacy of Ventilation
proposals.motivation: The drive for energy efficiency and carbon neutrality in buildings, coupled with heightened awareness of airborne infection risks post-pandemic, places conflicting demands on building operation. The default response to indoor air quality (IAQ) issues, especially for VOCs and pathogens, is often to increase ventilation, which can significantly increase energy consumption and carbon emissions. A systematic, evidence-based framework is needed to determine when ventilation is optimal versus when alternative strategies like air cleaning or source control are more effective and sustainable.
proposals.hypothesis: For a given indoor air contaminant profile and building context, an optimal control strategy exists that minimizes a combined risk-energy cost function, and this strategy will not prioritize increased ventilation in a significant subset of realistic scenarios.
proposals.method: We will develop a simulation-based optimization framework integrating CONTAM and EnergyPlus. The framework will define a multi-objective cost function weighting infection risk (using a Wells-Riley or dose-response model), VOC exposure risk (using toxicity indices), and energy consumption. We will model a range of control strategies: variable ventilation (DCV), in-duct photocatalytic oxidation (PCO) based on TiO2, portable air cleaners, and source control. A genetic algorithm or Bayesian optimization will be used to identify Pareto-optimal control sequences for different building types (office, school) and contaminant scenarios (VOC-dominant, pathogen-dominant, mixed).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, risk-based decision-making tool for building designers and operators to select IAQ control strategies. It will generate clear evidence and boundary conditions (e.g., contaminant types, source strengths, climate zones) under which alternative strategies outperform increased ventilation, contributing to both public health and sustainability goals.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies compare individual strategies, this proposal integrates infection risk, VOC exposure, and energy use into a single optimization framework to *systematically challenge* ventilation as the primary solution, directly leveraging the researcher's expertise in risk assessment, VOC/PCO, and intelligent control systems.

proposals.title: A Multi-Objective Control Framework for Building HVAC Systems to Simultaneously Minimize Infection Risk and Energy Consumption
proposals.motivation: The COVID-19 pandemic highlighted a critical conflict in building management: increasing ventilation to mitigate airborne infection risk drastically elevates energy consumption. With the building sector accounting for significant global energy use and emissions, and the persistent threat of respiratory pathogens, a paradigm shift from a trade-off to a co-optimization strategy is urgently needed. This research addresses a core challenge in sustainable and healthy built environments.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating real-time occupancy sensing, predictive infection risk models, and dynamic energy simulation into a unified control architecture, an HVAC system can be operated to maintain infection risk below a defined safety threshold while minimizing energy use, thereby inverting the perceived efficiency-prevention trade-off.
proposals.method: The research will develop and test a model predictive control (MPC) framework. The framework integrates: 1) a Wells-Riley-based infection risk model, modified with a dose-response function for different pathogens, using real-time CO2 and occupancy as proxies for pathogen concentration; 2) a calibrated building energy model (e.g., in EnergyPlus) for predictive energy simulation; and 3) a multi-objective optimization algorithm (e.g., NSGA-II) to generate Pareto-optimal control setpoints for outdoor air rates, filtration (MERV/HEPA), and air recirculation. The system will be simulated in a virtual testbed representing a public building zone.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantifiable methodology for the joint optimization of indoor air quality (IAQ) for health and energy efficiency. It will deliver a scalable control framework and actionable metrics (e.g., risk-adjusted energy efficiency ratio) for building operators. The results will advance the field of intelligent building operation beyond single-objective optimization.
proposals.novelty: While infection risk and energy have been studied separately, this proposal's novelty lies in the real-time, predictive integration of a probabilistic infection risk model directly into the MPC cost function, treating infection prevention as a dynamic constraint rather than a static setpoint. This moves beyond reviewing technologies (like TiO2) to creating an intelligent, adaptive control system.

proposals.title: A Model-Predictive Control Framework for Multi-Objective Ventilation Management: Integrating Infection Risk and VOC Exposure with Energy Efficiency
proposals.motivation: Building ventilation systems face the complex challenge of simultaneously mitigating airborne infection risk (e.g., from pathogens) and chemical exposure (e.g., VOCs) while minimizing energy consumption. Pre-2026, most control strategies are reactive or rule-based, lacking the anticipatory capability to optimize for these dynamic, often conflicting objectives in real-time. This gap limits the ability to ensure indoor air quality (IAQ) and occupant health efficiently, especially in public buildings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Model-Predictive Control (MPC) algorithm, adapted from advanced robotics for its precision in handling multi-variable, constrained systems, can dynamically optimize ventilation rates to maintain target infection risk and VOC concentration thresholds with lower energy use compared to conventional setpoint-based or demand-controlled ventilation.
proposals.method: We will develop a state-space model of a building zone, integrating dynamics of contaminant concentration (both bioaerosols and VOCs), occupancy, and energy use. A receding-horizon MPC algorithm, using Quadratic Programming for optimization, will be implemented. It will predictively minimize a cost function weighting energy consumption against constraint violations for IAQ metrics, using real-time or forecasted inputs of occupancy, outdoor air quality, and indoor source strength. The control output will be damper positions and fan speeds. Performance will be evaluated via simulation in TRNSYS/Contam and compared against baseline strategies (e.g., CO2-based DCV) using metrics of infection risk (Wells-Riley or dose-response), VOC concentration, and energy consumption.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, unified control framework that explicitly and simultaneously manages infection risk and VOC exposure. It will provide a quantitative methodology for trading off IAQ health objectives against energy efficiency in ventilation operation. The resulting control strategy is a direct step towards the 'intelligent operation' needed for resilient and sustainable public buildings.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the cross-disciplinary application of robust, multi-objective MPC from robotics to the specific, coupled problem of pathogen and VOC control, moving beyond single-pollutant or comfort-based approaches. This integrates Dr. Haghighat's expertise in VOC systems and infection risk into a single, anticipatory control paradigm.

proposals.title: A Bayesian Inference Framework for the Spatiotemporal Identification and Quantification of Indoor Pollutant Sources Using Sparse Sensor Networks
proposals.motivation: Effectively managing indoor air quality (IAQ) and mitigating infection risk requires precise knowledge of pollutant source locations and strengths. Current building management systems, while capable of measuring concentrations, lack the ability to automatically identify sources, leading to inefficient ventilation control and delayed responses to episodic releases of VOCs or bioaerosols. This gap is critical for public buildings, where rapid source identification can directly inform targeted ventilation strategies to reduce exposure and energy use.
proposals.hypothesis: That a physics-informed Bayesian inference framework, integrating a fast-response transport model with sparse, real-time concentration data, can accurately and robustly solve the inverse problem to locate and quantify transient indoor pollutant sources, including VOCs and infectious aerosols.
proposals.method: The research will develop a two-stage inverse modeling approach. First, a reduced-order building airflow and contaminant transport model (e.g., a multi-zone or fast CFD surrogate) will be constructed to serve as the forward model. Second, a Bayesian inference algorithm (specifically a Markov Chain Monte Carlo, MCMC, sampler) will be employed to estimate the posterior probability distribution of source parameters (location, release rate, start time) by assimilating time-series concentration data from a limited sensor network. The framework will be validated in a controlled environmental chamber with known VOC and tracer gas releases before a pilot study in a real public building zone.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, practical methodology for the intelligent, automated diagnosis of indoor air pollution events, moving beyond simple concentration monitoring. It will provide a foundational tool for next-generation building control systems that can transition from generalized dilution to source-targeted ventilation, thereby directly supporting the dual goals of infection risk mitigation and energy efficiency central to the researcher's recent work.
proposals.novelty: While inverse modeling is used in atmospheric sciences, its application for real-time, fine-grained source identification inside buildings using sparse, commodity-grade sensors is limited. The novelty lies in the tailored integration of a computationally efficient Bayesian framework with building-specific airflow physics to address the underdetermined nature of the indoor source identification problem.

proposals.title: Experimental Validation of a One-Dimensional Kinetic Model for Photocatalytic Oxidation of Indoor VOC Mixtures Using a Commercial Filter
proposals.motivation: Photocatalytic oxidation (PCO) is a promising technology for removing volatile organic compounds (VOCs) from indoor air, but its performance for realistic mixtures is complex and poorly predicted. Existing models, often validated for single VOCs, fail to account for competitive adsorption and reaction kinetics in mixtures, limiting their use for designing and controlling ventilation and air cleaning systems for real-world indoor environments.
proposals.hypothesis: A one-dimensional, multi-species Langmuir-Hinshelwood kinetic model, incorporating competitive adsorption parameters, can accurately predict the steady-state and transient removal efficiency of a binary VOC mixture (e.g., toluene and formaldehyde) by a commercial TiO2-based PCO filter under varying inlet concentrations and air flow rates.
proposals.method: A bench-scale test rig with controlled environmental parameters (UV intensity, humidity, temperature) will be used to collect experimental data for single and binary VOC challenges. The one-dimensional model, implemented in MATLAB/Simulink, will solve coupled mass conservation and reaction rate equations. Model parameters will be initially estimated from single-VOC experiments and literature. Validation will involve comparing model outputs (outlet concentration, removal efficiency) against experimental data for mixtures using statistical metrics (R-squared, normalized mean bias error) and sensitivity analysis on key kinetic parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, practical modeling tool for predicting PCO performance for VOC mixtures, directly informing the intelligent operation of building air cleaning systems. It will advance the field by quantifying model uncertainty and identifying critical parameters for mixture effects, bridging a gap between theoretical kinetics and applied building system design for indoor air quality control.
proposals.novelty: While 1D PCO models exist, their rigorous experimental validation for competitive multi-VOC kinetics using a commercially available filter is limited. This study uniquely integrates pre-2026 modeling approaches with systematic mixture experimentation to produce a validated tool ready for integration into building ventilation control algorithms.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Infection and Chronic Health Risks from Secondary By-Products of Photocatalytic Oxidation for Indoor VOC Control
proposals.motivation: Photocatalytic oxidation (PCO) is a promising technology for removing indoor VOCs, but pre-2026 reviews (including the researcher's own) highlight the generation of hazardous secondary by-products like formaldehyde, acetaldehyde, and ultrafine particles. The health risks of these by-products in real indoor environments, particularly their relative contribution to chronic toxicity versus acute infection risk via airway irritation, remain poorly quantified. This gap hinders the intelligent operation of PCO-integrated ventilation systems that aim to balance energy efficiency with occupant health protection.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for common indoor VOC mixtures, the integrated health risk from PCO-generated by-products—encompassing both long-term carcinogenic potential and short-term irritation that may increase infection susceptibility—can, under certain operational conditions, outweigh the risk reduction achieved by removing the primary VOCs.
proposals.method: The study will employ a combined experimental and modeling risk assessment framework. First, a bench-scale TiO2-PCO reactor will treat a standard mixture of VOCs (e.g., toluene, limonene) under different UV intensities and residence times, with by-product speciation using GC-MS and PTR-MS. Second, a multi-pathway health risk assessment will be conducted, calculating chronic cancer and non-cancer risks for key by-products (e.g., formaldehyde) and modeling transient concentrations. Third, a relative infection risk model, adapted from the researcher's prior work, will incorporate by-product-induced irritation as a modifying factor for respiratory infection probability. Finally, a Monte Carlo analysis will quantify uncertainty and identify critical control parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first integrated risk comparison framework for PCO technologies, quantifying trade-offs between primary VOC removal and secondary pollutant generation. It will deliver evidence-based guidelines for the safe and intelligent operation of PCO systems within building ventilation controls, directly contributing to the design of health-focused, energy-efficient indoor air quality management systems.
proposals.novelty: While by-product formation is known, this proposal uniquely integrates chronic toxicity metrics with a dynamic infection risk model to evaluate the net health impact of PCO operation, moving beyond concentration measurements alone to a comprehensive risk-based performance evaluation.

proposals.title: Performance and Risk Assessment of Hybrid Ventilation-Purification Systems in Buildings with Non-Ideal Heat Emission Systems
proposals.motivation: A significant portion of the existing building stock, especially in older or retrofitted structures, relies on poorly performing or non-traditional heat emission systems (e.g., underfloor heating with poor stratification, oversized radiators, or convective systems with high air movement). These non-ideal conditions can severely disrupt indoor airflow patterns and pollutant dispersion, undermining the effectiveness of advanced ventilation and air cleaning strategies designed for modern, ideal systems. Evaluating hybrid systems only under ideal design conditions provides an incomplete and overly optimistic picture of their real-world efficacy for infection control and VOC management.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the performance of hybrid ventilation-photocatalytic oxidation (PCO) systems, in terms of infection risk mitigation and VOC removal, will be significantly degraded in buildings with non-ideal heat emission systems compared to baseline predictions, and that this degradation can be quantified and mitigated through intelligent, adaptive control.
proposals.method: The research will employ a combined modeling and experimental approach. First, a validated CONTAM/EnergyPlus co-simulation model will be extended to integrate a TiO2-PCO reactor module and representative non-ideal emission system models (e.g., creating excessive drafts or poor thermal stratification). Second, a risk assessment framework, using a Wells-Riley model variant for infection probability and a concentration-based metric for VOC exposure, will be applied to simulate scenarios under different emission system faults and operating modes. Finally, an intelligent control algorithm, based on model predictive control (MPC), will be developed to dynamically adjust ventilation rates and PCO operation in response to simulated sensor feedback (CO2, TVOC, temperature gradients) to optimize the trade-off between infection risk, indoor air quality, and energy use in these non-ideal conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, realistic performance benchmark for hybrid air quality systems in the context of imperfect building infrastructure. It will deliver a validated simulation method for assessing infection and VOC risk in buildings with faulty or non-standard systems and propose a practical intelligent control strategy to enhance robustness, directly informing retrofit guidelines and operational protocols for public buildings.
proposals.novelty: While hybrid systems and PCO have been studied, their evaluation has largely assumed ideal thermal boundary conditions. This study explicitly integrates the disruptive influence of real-world, non-ideal heat emission systems into the performance and risk assessment framework, which is a critical yet overlooked factor for successful implementation in existing buildings.

proposals.title: A Dynamic Power-to-Heat Ratio Framework for Integrated Building Energy and Indoor Air Quality Control: Modeling, Simulation, and Risk-Energy Trade-off Analysis
proposals.motivation: Building energy systems are typically designed and operated with a constant power-to-heat ratio, which is a major simplification. Real building demands for electricity (e.g., for ventilation, cooling, lighting) and heat (e.g., for space heating, domestic hot water) vary dynamically on sub-hourly timescales due to occupancy, weather, and equipment use. This mismatch between static system assumptions and dynamic loads leads to energy inefficiency and can compromise indoor environmental quality (IEQ) control, such as the dilution of airborne contaminants like VOCs or pathogens.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling and optimizing building systems with a dynamically varying power-to-heat ratio will reveal significant opportunities for simultaneous energy savings and improved management of indoor air quality (IAQ) and infection risk, compared to conventional static-ratio operation.
proposals.method: We will develop a co-simulation framework integrating a detailed building energy model (EnergyPlus/TRNSYS) with an IAQ/infection risk model (using Wells-Riley or dose-response for pathogens, and mass balance for VOCs). The core will be a Model Predictive Control (MPC) algorithm that optimizes the dispatch of HVAC and on-site generation systems (e.g., heat pumps, CHP) by dynamically adjusting the power-to-heat ratio. The optimization objective will be a weighted function of energy cost and an IAQ risk index, evaluated on 15-minute timesteps using forecasted occupancy and weather data.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel modeling and control framework that explicitly links dynamic energy system operation with quantitative IAQ and infection risk metrics. It will provide a methodology to quantify the trade-offs between energy efficiency and health protection in buildings, moving beyond static design assumptions. The results will offer actionable control strategies for intelligent building operation systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit integration of a dynamically varying power-to-heat ratio within a multi-objective MPC framework that jointly optimizes for energy and real-time, concentration-based IAQ/infection risk, a synthesis not fully addressed in pre-2026 literature which often treats these objectives separately or with static ratios.

proposals.title: A Risk-Aware Control Framework for Hybrid CHP-Heat Pump Systems: Primary CHP Operation for Energy Efficiency with Dynamic Peak Boosting for Infection Risk Mitigation
proposals.motivation: Integrated building energy systems face the dual challenge of achieving carbon neutrality while ensuring indoor environmental health, particularly post-pandemic. Conventional designs often use heat pumps as the base load with Combined Heat and Power (CHP) for backup, potentially underutilizing the CHP's efficient cogeneration. Inverting this hierarchy could enhance overall system efficiency and resilience, but its impact on dynamic indoor air quality (IAQ) and infection risk under real-time occupancy and ventilation demands is unknown.
proposals.hypothesis: A system where a CHP unit serves as the primary, modulating heat source, supplemented by electric heat pumps for peak thermal and ventilation load boosting, will achieve lower annual carbon emissions and operational costs while maintaining or improving infection risk control compared to conventional designs, when managed by an intelligent, risk-informed control strategy.
proposals.method: The research will develop a model predictive control (MPC) framework co-optimizing energy use and infection risk. The system model integrates a natural gas CHP, air-source heat pumps, and the building HVAC system. The MPC cost function will weight energy cost (using real-time pricing) against a relative infection risk index, calculated from real-time CO2-based occupancy estimation and required ventilation rates per pre-2026 risk models. The algorithm will determine the optimal setpoints for CHP electrical/thermal output and the activation of heat pumps for peak boosting, using historical weather data and occupancy profiles for simulation in TRNSYS/EnergyPlus.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel control architecture for hybrid thermal systems that explicitly trades off energy efficiency and infection risk. It will yield a validated simulation case study demonstrating the annual performance of the inverted design, contributing a new operational paradigm to the fields of building energy systems and healthy indoor environments.
proposals.novelty: While CHP and heat pump integration has been studied, the proposed inversion of their primary/peak roles, governed by a control system that dynamically balances energy metrics with a quantitative infection risk index, represents a significant and unexplored shift in system design philosophy for public buildings.

proposals.title: A Model Predictive Control Framework for Hybrid HVAC Systems: Co-Optimizing Energy Efficiency and Indoor Air Quality via Dynamic Component Management
proposals.motivation: The decarbonization of building energy systems increasingly relies on hybrid systems combining Combined Heat and Power (CHP), heat pumps, and thermal storage. However, pre-2026 control strategies often prioritize energy efficiency alone, neglecting the dynamic impact of system operation on indoor air quality (IAQ), particularly ventilation rates and pollutant concentrations. This creates a critical gap, as suboptimal control can inadvertently increase infection risk or VOC exposure while trying to save energy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-objective Model Predictive Control (MPC) framework, which dynamically manages CHP, heat pumps, and storage based on real-time forecasts of energy demand and IAQ risk, can simultaneously achieve significant energy savings and maintain IAQ within health-based thresholds more effectively than conventional single-objective or rule-based strategies.
proposals.method: The method will develop a simulation-based MPC framework using a detailed building energy model (in EnergyPlus/TRNSYS) coupled with a multi-zone contaminant transport model for bioaerosols and VOCs. The control algorithm will utilize a receding horizon optimization with a cost function weighting energy use (from electricity/gas markets) against IAQ risk, quantified via infection risk models (Wells-Riley) and VOC concentration indices. Specific optimization will be performed using a mixed-integer linear programming (MILP) solver to handle the discrete and continuous states of the hybrid components. The system's performance will be evaluated under different climate and occupancy scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a validated, integrated control methodology for hybrid HVAC systems that explicitly trades off energy efficiency and IAQ. It will provide a quantitative framework for defining IAQ constraints in optimal control and deliver specific control sequences for hybrid components under varying operational priorities. The results will inform the design of next-generation intelligent building operation systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit integration of time-varying infection risk and VOC concentration models as dynamic constraints within a real-time MPC scheme for hybrid energy system control, moving beyond static setpoints or separate optimization of energy and IAQ systems prevalent in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: A Multi-Variable Sensitivity Analysis Framework for Optimizing Building Ventilation Systems: Integrating Climate, Energy Cost, and Typology Effects on Infection Risk and Energy Use
proposals.motivation: Current sensitivity analyses for building ventilation control systems, particularly those aimed at balancing infection risk mitigation and energy efficiency, often focus on a narrow set of operational parameters like power-to-heat ratios. This limits the generalizability of findings across diverse real-world contexts. Pre-2026, there is a pressing need to understand how broader, external variables—such as volatile energy prices, varying climate conditions, and fundamental building typologies—affect system performance and optimal control strategies to ensure robustness under uncertainty.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the sensitivity of optimal ventilation control strategies to energy prices and climate data will be greater than to internal system parameters for certain building types, and that the dominant variable influencing the trade-off between infection risk and energy efficiency will shift significantly across different building archetypes and climatic zones.
proposals.method: We will develop a simulation-based global sensitivity analysis framework using Monte Carlo methods and Sobol indices. The core model will integrate an infection risk model (e.g., a Wells-Riley-based approach) and an energy model within a building performance simulation environment (e.g., EnergyPlus/TRNSYS). The analysis will systematically vary three key variable sets: (1) climate data (using TMY files from diverse ASHRAE zones), (2) energy price trajectories (based on pre-2026 historical volatility), and (3) building type archetypes (office, school, retail). The control logic will be based on a rule-based or model predictive control (MPC) architecture to minimize a combined cost function of infection risk and energy cost.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a comprehensive map of the external drivers most critical for the design and operation of infection-resilient ventilation systems. It will yield practical guidelines for prioritizing climate adaptation and economic hedging strategies in building management. The proposed framework will serve as a decision-support tool for policymakers and building operators to develop robust control protocols applicable across a wider range of scenarios.
proposals.novelty: While sensitivity analysis is common, its application simultaneously to this triad of external, non-operational variables (climate, market prices, building typology) in the context of infection risk-energy trade-offs is novel. This moves beyond system-internal tuning to assess the vulnerability and adaptability of control strategies to real-world macroeconomic and environmental forces.

proposals.title: Model Predictive Control for Hybrid Ventilation and Air Purification Systems: Optimizing Indoor Air Quality and Energy Use in Public Buildings
proposals.motivation: The operation of hybrid HVAC systems, integrating mechanical ventilation with technologies like photocatalytic oxidation (PCO) for VOC removal, is complex. Current rule-based controls are reactive and fail to optimally balance competing objectives of infection risk mitigation (via ventilation) and energy efficiency under dynamic weather and occupancy conditions. A proactive, optimization-based control strategy is needed to navigate these trade-offs in real-time.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a Model Predictive Control (MPC) framework, which uses forecasts of weather, occupancy, and indoor pollutant generation, can optimize the dynamic operation of a hybrid ventilation-PCO system to simultaneously minimize infection risk (quantified via a rebreathed fraction or CO2-based proxy) and energy consumption, outperforming conventional control strategies.
proposals.method: We will develop a linear state-space model of a building zone, capturing dynamics of key indoor air quality (IAQ) indicators (CO2, target VOCs) and thermal energy. The MPC controller will solve a constrained finite-horizon optimization problem at each time step, using forecasts for outdoor conditions and internal loads. The cost function will penalize predicted infection risk (using a Wells-Riley model adapted for ventilation), VOC concentrations, and energy use (fan & conditioning). Control variables will be the supply airflow rate and the PCO system operational state. The optimization will be implemented using a receding horizon approach with a quadratic programming (QP) solver.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel control framework for integrated IAQ and energy management, demonstrating quantifiable improvements over baseline systems. It will deliver a methodology to explicitly trade off infection risk against energy costs in building operation, contributing directly to the design of intelligent building control systems for public health and sustainability.
proposals.novelty: While MPC is established in process engineering, its application to co-optimize airborne infection risk and VOC removal in a hybrid mechanical-nature-inspired (PCO) ventilation system is novel. The integration of a dynamic VOC removal model (based on pre-2026 PCO kinetics) with a ventilation-driven infection risk model within an MPC framework represents a significant advance over single-objective or single-technology control studies.

proposals.title: A Multi-Objective Optimization Framework for District-Scale Hybrid Ventilation and Heating Systems: Integrating Infection Risk Mitigation, VOC Removal, and Energy Efficiency
proposals.motivation: The decarbonization of building energy systems necessitates scaling up efficient solutions like hybrid ventilation with heat recovery. However, integrating such systems at a district scale introduces complex trade-offs between energy use for heating, indoor air quality (IAQ) regarding VOCs and pathogens, and operational stability across heterogeneous buildings. Pre-2026, most research optimizes single buildings; a systematic, scalable control framework for clusters is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: A centralized-distributed optimization architecture, coordinating individual building-level hybrid systems within a district heating network, can simultaneously minimize infection risk probability, maintain VOC concentrations below health guidelines, and reduce aggregate heating energy demand compared to independent building operations.
proposals.method: The method involves developing a digital twin of a building cluster using EnergyPlus/TRNSYS for thermal modeling and CONTAM for IAQ (VOC transport, pathogen dispersion via Wells-Riley model). A two-layer control architecture will be implemented: a lower layer uses model predictive control (MPC) in each building to manage ventilation rates and photocatalytic oxidation (PCO) unit activation based on local sensors. An upper layer employs a multi-agent deep reinforcement learning (DRL) algorithm, specifically a Multi-Agent Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (MADDPG), to coordinate thermal energy requests from the district network, optimizing for system-wide energy efficiency and peak load reduction while respecting IAQ constraints communicated from the lower layer.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, scalable control framework for district energy-IAQ management, validated through simulation. It will provide quantifiable trade-off curves (Pareto fronts) between infection risk, VOC exposure, and energy use for district operators. The integration of PCO effectiveness models into a district-scale optimization is a new contribution to the field.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit, simultaneous optimization of infection risk and VOC removal alongside thermal energy efficiency at the district scale, which is largely unexplored. The proposed hybrid MADDPG-MPC architecture for coordinating physical IAQ processes and thermal energy flows in a network of buildings is a non-obvious advancement beyond single-building intelligent control systems.

proposals.title: Development and Performance Evaluation of a Miniaturized, Intelligent Ventilation and Air Purification System for Individual Apartment Units
proposals.motivation: Centralized building systems often fail to address the heterogeneous and dynamic indoor air quality (IAQ) needs of individual apartments, leading to occupant exposure to VOCs, airborne infection risks, and energy inefficiency. The rise of remote work and heightened health awareness post-pandemic underscores the need for personalized, effective IAQ control at the apartment scale, which remains an underdeveloped application area. This research addresses the gap between whole-building solutions and portable air cleaners by proposing a dedicated, integrated system for single dwelling units.
proposals.hypothesis: A miniaturized, intelligent system integrating demand-controlled ventilation with a compact photocatalytic oxidation (PCO) unit can effectively mitigate VOC concentrations and airborne infection risk in individual apartments while maintaining energy efficiency comparable to centralized systems. The system's performance will be superior to standalone portable air purifiers due to the inclusion of controlled fresh air supply and real-time, multi-objective optimization.
proposals.method: The research will design and prototype a system comprising a small-scale energy recovery ventilator (ERV), a compact TiO2-based PCO reactor with UV-LEDs, and low-cost IAQ sensors (CO2, TVOC, PM2.5). An embedded control unit will execute a multi-objective optimization algorithm (e.g., a weighted cost function balancing infection risk, based on a Wells-Riley model adaptation, against energy consumption) to modulate ventilation rates and PCO operation. Performance will be evaluated in an environmental chamber simulating a studio apartment under different occupancy and pollution source scenarios, comparing IAQ parameters and energy use against baseline conditions and a commercial portable HEPA filter.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated design framework and performance data for apartment-scale intelligent IAQ systems, directly contributing to the field of personalized indoor environments. It will offer a novel methodology for real-time, risk-based control in decentralized settings and generate insights into the synergistic effects of hybrid ventilation and PCO purification at a reduced scale.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the system-level miniaturization and integration of demand-controlled ventilation with advanced air cleaning (PCO) specifically for the apartment unit, governed by a real-time optimization algorithm that jointly minimizes infection risk and energy use—a control challenge not addressed by standalone purifiers or centralized building HVAC.

proposals.title: Inverse Design of Building Envelopes and Load Profiles for Optimized Performance of Hybrid Ventilation and Air Purification Systems
proposals.motivation: Current building design often treats the HVAC and air cleaning systems as add-ons to a fixed envelope, leading to suboptimal energy and indoor air quality (IAQ) performance. The rise of hybrid systems combining demand-controlled ventilation with technologies like photocatalytic oxidation (PCO) for VOC removal creates a complex, interdependent system. An inverse design methodology is needed to determine the optimal building shell and internal load characteristics that maximize the efficiency and effectiveness of a predefined hybrid system configuration, addressing both infection risk mitigation and energy efficiency goals.
proposals.hypothesis: For a specified hybrid ventilation and air cleaning system, there exists a Pareto-optimal set of building envelope properties (e.g., thermal mass, airtightness, window-to-wall ratio) and internal load profiles that simultaneously minimize energy use and IAQ risk indices (e.g., infection probability, VOC concentration).
proposals.method: The research will employ a multi-objective optimization framework coupling a building energy model (EnergyPlus) with IAQ and risk assessment models (e.g., Wells-Riley for infection, mass balance for VOCs). The hybrid system's performance, including PCO reactor efficiency and ventilation control logic, will be embedded as fixed constraints. A genetic algorithm (NSGA-II) will be used to inversely solve for the optimal building parameters. The design variables will include envelope U-values, thermal mass, infiltration rate, and schedules for occupancy/equipment loads, while objectives will be annual energy consumption and a combined IAQ risk index.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel inverse design framework that explicitly links building passive properties to active system performance, moving beyond sequential design. It will yield practical guidelines for architects and engineers on how to tailor a building's shell for specific hybrid IAQ systems. The results will advance the field of integrated building system design for health and sustainability.
proposals.novelty: While forward simulation of system performance is common, inverting the problem to derive optimal building characteristics for a *given* hybrid system is a novel paradigm shift. It integrates pre-2026 risk-based IAQ assessment and multi-objective optimization specifically for the co-design of the building with technologies like PCO and intelligent ventilation control.

proposals.title: A Geotechnical and Environmental Suitability Model (GESM) for Compressed Air Energy Storage: An Ecological Niche Modeling Approach to Forecast Site Suitability and Cumulative Impact
proposals.motivation: The large-scale deployment of Compressed Air Energy Storage (CAES) is critical for grid stability with high renewable penetration, but site selection is complex and risks significant geological impacts and working fluid (e.g., air, potential additives) emissions. Current environmental assessments are reactive and site-specific, lacking a proactive, regional-scale framework to forecast cumulative risks, analogous to unplanned urban development leading to heat islands and VOC accumulation, which your prior work addresses.
proposals.hypothesis: That the core algorithms of Species Distribution Models (SDMs), specifically Maximum Entropy (MaxEnt) and Generalized Linear Models (GLMs), can be successfully adapted to integrate geotechnical, hydrological, and land-use variables to create a predictive model that accurately forecasts CAES site suitability and its associated cumulative environmental impact at a regional scale.
proposals.method: We will adapt the MaxEnt algorithm, a proven SDM method, to create the GESM. Input variables will include geological formation integrity (from seismic surveys), aquifer depth/protection, seismic risk maps, surface land use (CORINE data), and proximity to grid infrastructure. 'Presence' data for model training will be known suitable CAES sites (e.g., Huntorf, McIntosh) and analogous geological formations. The model will output a spatially-explicit suitability and risk index, followed by a lifecycle analysis module to estimate cumulative working fluid emission risks using a Gaussian plume dispersion model for different deployment scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first predictive, spatially-explicit tool for proactive CAES zoning, directly contributing to sustainable energy infrastructure planning. It will introduce a novel methodology for cumulative impact assessment in geo-energy systems, bridging conservation biology and energy engineering. The risk framework for working fluid emissions extends your expertise in indoor air quality and VOC control to the outdoor, industrial scale.
proposals.novelty: The direct conceptual and methodological translation of ecological niche modeling (MaxEnt) to geo-energy site suitability is novel. Unlike standard GIS overlay analyses, this approach provides a probabilistic, machine-learning-based predictive model trained on complex variable interactions, specifically designed to forecast cumulative impacts from widespread deployment.

proposals.title: A Risk-Aware Model Predictive Control Framework for Compressed Air Energy Storage Integrating Real-Time Geomechanical Observability
proposals.motivation: Compressed Air Energy Storage (CAES) is critical for grid-scale renewable energy integration, but its operation in salt caverns or porous rock formations poses significant geomechanical risks, including fatigue, fracturing, and potential loss of integrity. Current operational strategies lack a real-time, predictive coupling between dynamic grid demands and the evolving state of the geological asset, leading to overly conservative operation or unquantified long-term risk. This gap hinders the safe, optimal, and economically viable deployment of CAES.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a real-time geomechanical observability module, using techniques like Distributed Acoustic Sensing (DAS) and passive seismic monitoring, into a Model Predictive Control (MPC) architecture will enable risk-optimized CAES operation that dynamically balances grid flexibility targets with the quantified probability of cavern failure or fatigue.
proposals.method: The research will develop a co-simulation framework linking a high-fidelity, reduced-order geomechanical model of the storage cavern (informed by pre-2026 CCS integrity monitoring literature) with a dynamic process model of the CAES thermodynamic cycle. A risk quantification module will translate geomechanical model outputs (e.g., stress, strain, microseismic event rate) into a dynamic, probabilistic constraint for the MPC's optimization problem. The MPC algorithm will then solve for optimal charge/discharge trajectories that minimize a combined cost function of energy market penalties and geomechanical risk, using a moving horizon estimation approach to update model states from the sensor data stream.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, integrated control-observability architecture for geo-energy systems, advancing beyond static safety factors. It will deliver a methodology to quantify and monetize operational risk in CAES, enabling safer, more aggressive, and more valuable asset utilization. The framework contributes directly to the secure integration of variable renewables by enhancing the flexibility and reliability of large-scale storage.
proposals.novelty: The synthesis of real-time geophysical monitoring data—common in CCS—with a dynamic, economic process control algorithm from chemical engineering represents a significant cross-disciplinary fusion not yet applied to CAES. The core innovation is the formulation of geomechanical risk as a dynamic, probabilistic constraint within a real-time optimization loop, moving from post-hoc analysis to predictive, risk-aware control.

proposals.title: A Hybrid HVAC Control System Integrating Photocatalytic Foam Filters for Concurrent VOC Degradation and Infection Risk Mitigation
proposals.motivation: Indoor air quality (IAQ) management faces dual challenges: mitigating airborne infection risks and controlling volatile organic compound (VOC) concentrations. Conventional HVAC systems struggle to address both simultaneously, often leading to energy-intensive solutions like high ventilation rates. A multi-pollutant control strategy is needed to improve occupant health while maintaining energy efficiency, aligning with public health and carbon neutrality goals.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a TiO2-coated photocatalytic open-cell foam filter into an HVAC system, governed by an intelligent control algorithm, will simultaneously reduce the concentration of key VOCs and the relative infection risk of airborne pathogens in an energy-efficient manner compared to baseline ventilation strategies.
proposals.method: We will design and fabricate a replaceable filter cartridge using a TiO2-coated alumina foam substrate, optimizing coating density and foam geometry for low airflow resistance. This filter will be integrated into a lab-scale HVAC test rig. A model predictive control (MPC) algorithm will be developed, using real-time sensor inputs for total VOC concentration and CO2 (as a proxy for occupancy/risk). The MPC will dynamically adjust the photocatalytic filter's UV-A activation and the system's outdoor air damper position to minimize a combined cost function of infection risk (using a Wells-Riley model) and energy consumption.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, integrated hardware and control solution for multi-pollutant IAQ management. It will deliver a quantitative framework for trading off VOC removal, infection risk reduction, and energy use, contributing directly to the design of next-generation, health-focused building ventilation systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the co-design of a novel, low-pressure-drop photocatalytic filter form-factor with an intelligent control system that explicitly and dynamically optimizes for both VOC degradation and infection risk parameters, moving beyond single-pollutant or static design approaches.

proposals.title: Machine Learning-Driven Optimization of Hydrothermal Synthesis for Enhanced TiO2 Photocatalytic VOC Degradation in Indoor Air
proposals.motivation: TiO2 photocatalysis is a promising technology for removing indoor volatile organic compounds (VOCs), a key factor for occupant health and indoor air quality. However, the photocatalytic performance is highly sensitive to synthesis parameters and doping, making empirical optimization inefficient. A data-driven approach is needed to systematically navigate the complex synthesis space and accelerate the development of high-performance, energy-efficient air purification materials.
proposals.hypothesis: Machine learning models can accurately predict the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of TiO2-based materials for a target VOC mixture from hydrothermal synthesis conditions and doping levels, enabling the inverse design of optimal synthesis protocols.
proposals.method: We will compile a curated dataset from published literature (pre-2026) on TiO2 photocatalysts synthesized hydrothermally, including features like precursor type, temperature, time, pH, dopant identity/concentration, and characterization data (e.g., band gap, surface area). The target variable will be photocatalytic performance metrics (e.g., degradation rate, quantum yield) for a standard VOC mixture. We will train and compare several regression models, including Gradient Boosting Machines (XGBoost) and Multi-layer Perceptrons, using cross-validation. Feature importance analysis (SHAP) will identify critical synthesis parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a predictive framework for rational photocatalyst design, reducing costly trial-and-error in the lab. It will contribute to the development of more effective, tailored materials for integration into intelligent ventilation and air cleaning systems, directly supporting infection risk mitigation and energy efficiency goals in building operations.
proposals.novelty: While ML has been used for catalyst discovery, its application specifically to optimize the hydrothermal synthesis process for indoor air purification TiO2, linking detailed synthesis conditions directly to VOC degradation performance for building applications, is novel. This bridges materials science with building system engineering.

proposals.title: A Cross-City Validation of a Clustering and Bayesian Regression Framework for Predicting Indoor Air Quality and Infection Risk in Diverse Climatic and Urban Contexts
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on intelligent building control for infection risk mitigation and energy efficiency, such as Dr. Haghighat's recent work, is often validated in specific, localized contexts. The generalizability of data-driven frameworks linking urban form, ventilation systems, and indoor environmental quality (IEQ) to health risks across different climates and urban densities remains untested. Validating such models in diverse cities is crucial for developing robust, globally applicable smart building operation strategies that balance health protection and sustainability.
proposals.hypothesis: The clustering and Bayesian regression framework developed for predicting indoor contaminant (e.g., VOCs, bioaerosols) concentration and relative infection risk in one urban context will retain predictive accuracy when applied to other cities, but will require recalibration of key parameters related to local climate, building stock, and occupant behavior.
proposals.method: We will apply the established two-stage methodology. First, k-means clustering will be used to categorize districts in three target cities (e.g., a cold dense city, a hot-humid sprawling city, a temperate city) based on pre-2026 urban data (morphology, traffic, green space). Second, hierarchical Bayesian regression models will be built, using city-specific priors informed by local climate data and building ventilation system audits, to predict indoor PM2.5, VOC levels, and a relative infection risk index for respiratory pathogens. Model performance will be compared against city-specific monitored IEQ data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first multi-city validation of an integrated urban-climate-building-health modeling framework. It will yield transferable insights into which model parameters are universal and which require localization, directly informing the design of adaptable intelligent building control systems.
