The results will advance the field of sustainable urban health by offering a scalable methodology for city-specific IEQ and risk assessment.
proposals.novelty: While clustering and Bayesian methods are known, their combined application for cross-contextual prediction of coupled indoor air quality and infection risk—explicitly linking external urban clusters to internal system performance—is novel. This moves beyond single-city case studies to establish a methodology for contextual adaptation of smart building strategies.

proposals.title: An Agent-Based Modeling Framework for Simulating Adaptive Household Water Use Behavior Under Climate-Induced Water Stress
proposals.motivation: Climate change is intensifying water scarcity, placing stress on municipal systems and public health. Understanding how households adapt their water use under stress is critical for designing resilient water infrastructure and conservation policies. Pre-2026, while building systems for infection risk and VOCs are studied, the behavioral dynamics of water use, a key determinant of system demand, remain underexplored with computational social science methods.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that household water use adaptation under scarcity is a non-linear function of socio-economic attributes, perceived risk, and peer influence, and that agent-based modeling can reveal emergent conservation patterns and tipping points not captured by aggregate models.
proposals.method: We will develop an agent-based model (ABM) using the NetLogo or Mesa framework. Household agents will be endowed with attributes (income, property type, household size) and behavioral rules derived from survey data and Theory of Planned Behavior. Decision rules for water use (e.g., garden irrigation, appliance use) will be conditioned on dynamic signals: water pricing tiers, drought severity alerts, and observed neighbor behavior. The model will be calibrated and validated against municipal water consumption data.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel computational framework linking micro-scale behavioral decisions to macro-scale water demand, aiding in stress-testing infrastructure. It will provide policymakers with a tool to evaluate the efficacy of different conservation incentives (e.g., pricing, messaging) under projected climate scenarios, bridging the gap between engineering systems and human factors.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying agent-based modeling, common in infection dynamics, to the domain of residential water systems, and integrating risk perception—a core theme from the researcher's recent work—as a key driver of agent behavior within a coupled human-water system model.

proposals.title: A High-Resolution Forecasting Framework for Occupant-Centric Ventilation Control: Integrating Smart Meter Data, Infection Risk, and VOC Exposure at the Household Level
proposals.motivation: Current building-level ventilation and air quality management systems lack the granularity to address heterogeneous occupant schedules and pollutant generation patterns within a building, leading to simultaneous under-ventilation (increasing infection and VOC exposure risks) and energy waste. The proliferation of high-resolution smart meter data for electricity, gas, and water presents a novel, non-intrusive proxy for real-time occupancy and activity estimation at the household or room level, a scale critical for personalized health protection. This gap is significant for designing next-generation demand-controlled ventilation (DCV) systems that must balance infection risk mitigation (e.g., airborne pathogens) with VOC removal and energy efficiency, as highlighted in recent reviews of photocatalytic oxidation and intelligent building control systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a forecasting framework trained on high-resolution smart meter data can accurately predict dynamic occupancy and activity patterns at the household/room scale, and that integrating these forecasts with multi-pollutant (pathogen and VOC) concentration models will enable a predictive control strategy that significantly reduces integrated health risk (quantified via relative risk metrics) compared to standard DCV, without increasing energy consumption.
proposals.method: The method will first employ sequence-to-sequence models (e.g., LSTMs or Temporal Convolutional Networks) on sub-hourly smart meter data to forecast occupancy states and activity intensity. These forecasts will drive two parallel, validated indoor air quality models: a Wells-Riley-based model for airborne infection risk and a mass-balance model for VOC concentrations from common household products. A multi-objective optimization algorithm (e.g., NSGA-II) will then generate Pareto-optimal ventilation setpoint schedules 24-hours ahead, minimizing a weighted sum of predicted infection risk and VOC exposure relative to benchmarks, subject to energy constraints. The framework will be tested using simulated data for a neighborhood of heterogeneous households.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, data-driven predictive control framework that bridges smart meter analytics with indoor air quality science for occupant-centric building operation. It will provide a quantitative methodology to evaluate the trade-offs between different health risks (infection vs. chronic chemical exposure) at a previously unattainable spatial resolution. The outcome will be a scalable blueprint for integrating non-intrusive sensing into next-generation intelligent building operation and maintenance systems for simultaneous health protection and energy efficiency.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of smart meter data forecasting specifically for multi-pollutant health risk prediction at the sub-building scale, moving beyond energy or single-pollutant objectives. This approach non-obviously applies the 'infection risk mitigation' framework from public buildings to the heterogeneous household environment, while explicitly co-optimizing it with VOC removal targets informed by photocatalytic oxidation research.

proposals.title: A Hybrid Physics-Informed Machine Learning Framework for Short-Term Forecasting of Water Quality in Building Plumbing Systems
proposals.motivation: Legionella pneumophila and other opportunistic pathogens in building water systems pose a significant public health risk, with outbreaks linked to premise plumbing. Current water safety management relies on periodic manual sampling, which is reactive and fails to capture dynamic water quality changes influenced by stagnation, temperature, and disinfectant decay. Real-time, predictive operational control is needed to proactively mitigate microbial and chemical risks, aligning with the growing focus on infection risk mitigation in built environments.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a simplified hydraulic and water quality model with a recurrent neural network (RNN) trained on real-time sensor data (e.g., residual chlorine, temperature, flow) will enable accurate hourly to weekly forecasts of key risk indicators, allowing for predictive operational interventions.
proposals.method: We will develop a hybrid model coupling a physics-based mass-balance module for disinfectant decay and microbial regrowth with a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network to capture complex, non-linear temporal patterns from sensor data. The model will be trained and validated using high-frequency (e.g., 15-minute) data streams simulated from a well-calibrated EPANET-MSX model of a representative building plumbing network, incorporating stochastic water demand profiles. The forecasting performance will be evaluated against a pure LSTM baseline using metrics like RMSE for disinfectant concentration and F1-score for predicting risk threshold breaches.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel operational tool for predictive water safety management in buildings, moving from static risk assessment to dynamic forecasting. It will demonstrate the transfer of hybrid modeling concepts from HVAC and indoor air quality (IAQ) control to water distribution, contributing to the intelligent operation of building utility systems. The framework will establish a methodology for integrating domain knowledge with data-driven models for short-term forecasts in networked environmental systems.
proposals.novelty: While hybrid models exist for forecasting in ventilation and energy systems, their application to real-time, building-scale water quality forecasting is novel. The approach uniquely adapts methods from infection risk mitigation in airborne contexts (e.g., dynamic ventilation control) to the waterborne pathogen context, creating a predictive analog for plumbing system operation.

proposals.title: A Hybrid Transfer Learning and Physics-Informed Neural Network Framework for Predicting Indoor Air Quality and Infection Risk with Minimal Historical Data
proposals.motivation: Current data-driven models for indoor air quality (IAQ) and airborne infection risk require extensive, building-specific historical data, which is often unavailable for new or retrofitted buildings. This data scarcity hinders the intelligent, real-time control of ventilation systems for balancing energy efficiency with health objectives like VOC mitigation and infection risk reduction, a key challenge identified in recent research.
proposals.hypothesis: A hybrid modeling framework combining physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) with transfer learning from a pre-trained source domain can accurately predict critical IAQ parameters (e.g., VOC concentration, infection risk probability) for a target building using only a short period of operational data.
proposals.method: The method involves two stages. First, a source model (a PINN) will be trained on a large, diverse synthetic dataset generated from well-established physical models of contaminant transport (VOCs, bioaerosols) and HVAC dynamics. Second, for a target building, this pre-trained model will be fine-tuned via transfer learning using a short-term (e.g., one-week) dataset of measured CO2, VOCs, occupancy, and system operation. Specific algorithms include TensorFlow/PyTorch for implementing the PINN, which encodes mass-balance equations as a loss function, and domain adaptation techniques (e.g., feature alignment) for the transfer learning step.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical tool for rapid deployment of model-predictive control in buildings lacking long-term data, directly supporting infection risk mitigation and VOC management. It will advance the field of smart building operation by demonstrating how physics-based knowledge can compensate for data scarcity, making intelligent control more universally applicable.
proposals.novelty: While PINNs and transfer learning exist separately, their integration for IAQ prediction under extreme data limitation is novel. This approach moves beyond purely statistical or purely physical models by creating a hybrid that is both generalizable (via physics) and adaptable (via short-term data).

proposals.title: A Digital Twin Framework for Real-Time, Adaptive Control of Building Ventilation Systems to Balance Dynamic Infection Risk and Energy Use
proposals.motivation: Current building operation models often assume static conditions, failing to adapt to dynamic changes like occupancy shifts, system retrofits (e.g., adding air cleaners), or fluctuating pollution sources. This limits their ability to simultaneously optimize for critical, time-varying objectives such as airborne infection risk mitigation and energy efficiency, a challenge highlighted during the COVID-19 pandemic and in pursuit of carbon neutrality.
proposals.hypothesis: A digital twin framework that integrates real-time sensor data with adaptive control algorithms can dynamically reconfigure ventilation and air treatment systems to maintain target infection risk levels (e.g., using Wells-Riley or dose-response models) under changing conditions, while reducing energy consumption compared to static or rule-based operation.
proposals.method: We will develop a building digital twin using a modular, physics-informed data architecture. The core is a reinforcement learning (RL) agent, trained on EnergyPlus simulations co-simulated with a contaminant transport model. The agent's state space will include real-time occupancy (from CO2 or Wi-Fi data), indoor/outdoor VOC and PM2.5 concentrations, and system status. The action space will control HVAC setpoints and auxiliary systems (e.g., photocatalytic oxidizer operation from the researcher's review). Reward functions will penalize high infection risk (calculated via a relative risk index) and energy use. The model will be validated in a test chamber with simulated retrofit scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated methodological framework for the real-time, co-optimization of indoor air quality (IAQ) and energy objectives in dynamically operated buildings. It will deliver specific control strategies that adapt to system changes, contributing directly to intelligent building operation for public health and sustainability. The resulting digital twin prototype will be a tool for testing retrofit impacts.
proposals.novelty: While RL has been explored for building control, its integration with real-time, physics-based infection risk models and explicit accommodation of system retrofits (like adding photocatalytic oxidation) within a digital twin for adaptive operation is novel. This moves beyond static risk assessments or single-objective optimization.

proposals.title: End-to-End Deep Learning for Automated Feature Extraction from Building Data Streams to Predict Indoor Air Quality and Infection Risk
proposals.motivation: Current models for indoor air quality (IAQ) and airborne infection risk prediction rely heavily on manual feature engineering from building management system (BMS) data, which is time-consuming, expert-dependent, and sub-optimal. This limits the scalability and adaptability of intelligent ventilation control systems, especially for dynamic objectives like VOC removal and pathogen mitigation highlighted in recent research. An automated approach is needed to handle the complexity and high dimensionality of raw BMS and sensor data streams.
proposals.hypothesis: Deep neural networks can automatically learn optimal, task-specific feature representations directly from raw, multivariate building operation data streams, leading to more accurate and robust models for predicting critical indoor environmental metrics (e.g., VOC concentration, infection risk probability) compared to models using handcrafted features.
proposals.method: We will develop a hybrid deep learning architecture combining 1D Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) for local pattern extraction from time-series sensor data (e.g., CO2, VOCs, temperature, airflow) and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks for modeling temporal dependencies. This model will be trained end-to-end using labeled datasets from previous studies on VOC dynamics and infection risk. The performance will be benchmarked against state-of-the-art models using engineered features (e.g., air change rate, occupancy estimates) for predicting target variables like real-time infection risk (using a Wells-Riley model proxy) and VOC concentration spikes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, generalizable framework for building data analytics that reduces dependency on domain-specific feature engineering. It will deliver a more adaptive model for integrated IAQ and health risk prediction, directly supporting the intelligent operation and control of ventilation systems for energy efficiency and health protection, as aligned with the researcher's recent themes.
proposals.novelty: While deep learning is used in building energy forecasting, its application for end-to-end feature learning specifically targeting the joint prediction of infection risk and VOC dynamics from raw BMS streams is novel. This approach moves beyond using deep networks as mere black-box predictors by explicitly framing them as replacements for the manual feature engineering pipeline in IAQ science.

proposals.title: A Hybrid Deep Learning Framework for Multi-Objective Building Energy Prediction Integrating Indoor Air Quality Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Accurate building energy prediction is critical for grid stability and achieving carbon neutrality goals. However, existing models often neglect the dynamic coupling between energy use and indoor environmental quality (IEQ), particularly under scenarios like demand-controlled ventilation for VOC management or infection risk mitigation, which can significantly alter energy profiles.
proposals.hypothesis: A hybrid deep learning model that jointly captures the temporal dependencies of energy loads and key indoor air parameters (e.g., CO2, VOC concentrations) will yield more robust and physically consistent energy predictions than models trained on energy data alone, especially during transient operational events.
proposals.method: We will develop a hybrid encoder-decoder architecture. A Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network will encode multivariate time-series data (energy, weather, occupancy, CO2, selected VOCs). A parallel 1D Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) will extract local temporal features from high-frequency sensor data. These encoded features will be fused and decoded to predict short-term (24-hour) building energy consumption. The model will be trained and validated using high-resolution datasets from instrumented public buildings.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel data-driven modeling framework that explicitly links energy prediction with IAQ dynamics, providing a tool for simulating trade-offs in integrated building control systems. It will advance the field beyond single-objective prediction towards holistic building performance assessment for energy efficiency and health.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the hybrid LSTM-CNN architecture designed for multi-objective temporal learning, specifically integrating IAQ state variables as model inputs for energy prediction, which is a departure from conventional energy-only models and aligns with the researcher's focus on integrated ventilation and control systems.

proposals.title: A Human-Centric Digital Twin for Indoor Air Quality and Infection Risk Prediction: Integrating High-Resolution Occupant Behavior and Ventilation Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Current models for indoor air quality (IAQ) and airborne infection risk, such as those based on the Wells-Riley equation, often rely on simplified or static assumptions about occupancy and human activity. This limits their predictive accuracy in real-world, dynamic environments like offices and schools, where occupant-driven variations in CO2, VOCs, and pathogen generation significantly impact ventilation system performance and health outcomes. A more granular, behaviorally-informed model is needed to optimize intelligent building control systems for both energy efficiency and health protection, a core challenge identified in pre-2026 research.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating high-resolution, time-series data on occupant presence, activity levels, and personal control actions (e.g., window opening, local fan use) into a coupled IAQ-infection risk model will significantly improve the accuracy of predicting transient pollutant concentrations and relative infection risk compared to models using only scheduled or averaged occupancy.
proposals.method: We will develop a co-simulation framework linking a calibrated EnergyPlus model for building physics with a Python-based IAQ/risk agent-based model. Occupant behavior data (count, location, metabolic rate, and interaction with building systems) will be sourced from anonymized Wi-Fi/Bluetooth sensing, wearable devices, and environmental loggers. These features will be processed using a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network to predict short-term behavior patterns. The output will drive a dynamic mass-balance model for key VOCs and bioaerosols, with risk calculated via a time-dependent Wells-Riley approach. Model validation will use controlled chamber data and field measurements from existing instrumented testbeds.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, open-source digital twin framework that explicitly quantifies the impact of human behavioral heterogeneity on IAQ and infection risk. It will deliver a validated methodology for integrating real-time occupant data into building control algorithms, advancing the field beyond static or schedule-based assumptions. The results will provide actionable insights for the design of next-generation, human-in-the-loop ventilation systems that are responsive to actual, rather than presumed, indoor environmental demands.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have incorporated simple occupancy counts, this proposal uniquely fuses high-fidelity, multi-modal behavioral sensing with a coupled mechanistic model of VOC dynamics and infection risk, creating a predictive digital twin that adapts to observed human actions rather than predefined schedules.

proposals.title: A Transfer Learning Framework for Data-Efficient Modeling of Indoor Air Quality and Infection Risk in New Buildings
proposals.motivation: Data-driven models for building systems, such as those predicting VOC dynamics or airborne infection risk, require extensive, building-specific data for training, which is costly and time-consuming to acquire for new or renovated buildings. This creates a significant barrier to the rapid deployment of intelligent control systems for health and energy efficiency. A method to leverage existing data from instrumented 'source' buildings to bootstrap models for 'target' buildings with minimal new data is urgently needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a domain adaptation framework, using pre-2026 deep learning architectures, can effectively transfer knowledge of indoor environmental dynamics (e.g., VOC decay, particle dispersion) from a source building to a target building, achieving accurate predictions with less than 20% of the target data required for training a model from scratch.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage framework. First, a base model (e.g., a Temporal Convolutional Network or LSTM) will be pre-trained on high-resolution time-series data (VOC concentrations, CO2, occupancy, HVAC states) from a well-instrumented source building. Second, for a new target building, we will employ a Domain Adversarial Neural Network (DANN) or a fine-tuning approach with gradient reversal layers to adapt the model's feature representations. The adaptation will use a small, newly-collected dataset from the target, aligning the feature distributions between source and target domains to mitigate discrepancies in building geometry, usage patterns, and system characteristics.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a practical, data-efficient methodology for deploying IAQ and risk models in new buildings, accelerating the implementation of intelligent building control systems. It will provide new insights into which features of building environmental dynamics are transferable and which require building-specific calibration, advancing the field of applied transfer learning for built environments.
proposals.novelty: While transfer learning is established in computer vision, its application to spatio-temporal physical systems like buildings, specifically for joint IAQ and infection risk prediction, is novel. The integration of domain adversarial training with temporal deep learning models for this cross-building transfer problem represents a significant advance over simple model fine-tuning or rule-based approaches.

proposals.title: A Meta-Learning Framework for Rapid Adaptation of Indoor Air Quality and Infection Risk Prediction Models to New Building Environments
proposals.motivation: Building-specific models for indoor air quality (IAQ) and airborne infection risk are crucial for intelligent ventilation control but require extensive, costly site-specific data for training. This creates a significant barrier to deploying adaptive systems in diverse buildings, especially for rapid response to new pathogens or occupancy changes. A method to leverage knowledge from existing buildings to bootstrap accurate models in new ones with minimal data is urgently needed for scalable, resilient building operations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model-agnostic meta-learning (MAML) framework, pre-trained on heterogeneous building data, can enable rapid adaptation of IAQ and risk prediction models to a new building using only a short period of fine-tuning data, achieving performance comparable to models trained from scratch on extensive datasets.
proposals.method: We will develop a MAML-based framework using recurrent neural networks (RNNs) or temporal convolutional networks (TCNs) as base learners. The meta-learner will be trained on a diverse dataset of building time-series (VOC concentrations, CO2, occupancy, ventilation rates, infection risk indices) from multiple structures. The inner-loop optimization will learn to quickly adjust model parameters for a specific building, while the outer-loop updates the meta-initialization. Adaptation performance will be tested on held-out buildings, comparing fine-tuning speed and accuracy against conventional transfer learning and from-scratch training.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel computational framework that reduces the data and time cost of deploying building-specific IAQ/risk models, directly supporting the practical implementation of intelligent ventilation control systems. It will provide validated protocols for knowledge transfer between buildings, advancing the field of adaptive built environment management towards greater scalability and resilience.
proposals.novelty: While meta-learning has been explored in other domains, its application to the fast adaptation of indoor environmental quality models, specifically bridging the gap between heterogeneous physical systems (buildings) for joint VOC and infection risk prediction, is a novel and non-obvious integration of AI methodology with building science.

proposals.title: Interpretable Energy Prediction for Intelligent Building Control: A Symbolic Regression Approach to Balance Infection Risk and HVAC Efficiency
proposals.motivation: Intelligent building control systems, crucial for mitigating airborne infection risk while maintaining energy efficiency, often rely on complex, black-box models (e.g., deep neural networks). These models lack interpretability, hindering their trustworthiness, regulatory acceptance, and the ability to extract fundamental physical insights for robust system design. An interpretable modeling framework is urgently needed to provide actionable equations for real-time control and decision-making in ventilation system operation.
proposals.hypothesis: Symbolic regression can discover compact, physically plausible equations that predict building energy consumption with accuracy comparable to black-box models, while explicitly revealing the functional relationships between energy use, key indoor air quality (IAQ) parameters (e.g., CO2, VOC concentrations), infection risk indicators, and outdoor conditions.
proposals.method: We will apply genetic programming-based symbolic regression (e.g., using the gplearn library or PySR) to high-resolution datasets from public buildings, incorporating features such as occupancy, indoor/outdoor temperature, humidity, CO2, selected VOC concentrations, and estimated infection risk (e.g., using Wells-Riley or dose-response models). The algorithm will be tasked with evolving parsimonious mathematical expressions to predict HVAC energy use. Multi-objective optimization will balance prediction accuracy (RMSE) with model complexity (expression length) to prevent overfitting and ensure interpretability.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver transparent, equation-based models for energy prediction that can be directly integrated into interpretable control logic for building management systems. It will advance the field beyond black-box AI by providing causal insights into the energy-IAQ-risk trade-off, supporting the development of next-generation standards and control strategies for healthy, low-energy buildings.
proposals.novelty: While symbolic regression has been used in energy engineering, its novel application here is the explicit integration of dynamic infection risk metrics and VOC concentration data as model inputs to derive interpretable control laws for the dual objectives of health and efficiency, a synthesis not yet explored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: A Hybrid Physics-Informed Deep Learning Model for Sub-Minute Building Energy Load Prediction to Enable Real-Time Ventilation and Infection Risk Control
proposals.motivation: The push for energy-efficient, healthy buildings requires dynamic control of HVAC systems, especially to mitigate airborne infection risks as highlighted in recent research. Current building energy prediction models operate at hourly or longer intervals, which are too coarse for real-time adjustments to sudden occupancy changes or pollutant/VOC spikes. Ultra-short-term prediction at minute or second granularity is essential for intelligent control systems to balance energy efficiency with immediate indoor air quality (IAQ) and infection risk targets.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a physics-based model of building thermal dynamics and contaminant transport with a deep learning sequence model (e.g., Temporal Convolutional Network or Transformer) will significantly improve the accuracy and robustness of sub-hourly energy load predictions compared to purely data-driven approaches, especially during transient events.
proposals.method: We will develop a hybrid model where a reduced-order physical model (e.g., a resistance-capacitance network for thermal zones and a well-mixed model for VOC/infectious aerosol concentration) provides a foundational prediction. This output, along with high-frequency time-series data from building automation systems (occupancy counts, CO2, VOCs, outdoor conditions), will be fed into a deep learning architecture (e.g., a Temporal Convolutional Network for computational efficiency or an attention-based model) to learn and correct for residual errors and complex, non-linear interactions. The model will be trained and validated using high-resolution (≤1-minute) data from an instrumented testbed.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel modeling framework for ultra-short-term building energy forecasting, directly linking it to IAQ and infection risk metrics. It will contribute a publicly available, high-resolution dataset for the research community and demonstrate a control-oriented prediction tool that can be integrated into the intelligent operation systems studied by the researcher, enabling simultaneous optimization for energy and health.
proposals.novelty: While hybrid modeling exists for hourly prediction, its application to sub-minute granularity for joint energy and infection risk prediction is novel. The tight coupling of a contaminant transport model with a high-frequency energy predictor specifically for real-time control is an unexplored synthesis of the researcher's expertise in VOCs, infection risk, and intelligent systems.

proposals.title: A Hybrid ML-Ventilation Framework for Predictive Control of Indoor Pollutant Mixtures: Balancing Infection Risk and VOC Exposure
proposals.motivation: Modern buildings face complex indoor air quality challenges from dynamic mixtures of bioaerosols and volatile organic compounds (VOCs). Pre-2026, building management systems lack adaptive intelligence to optimize ventilation and air cleaning parameters for novel, mixed pollutant scenarios, leading to a trade-off between infection risk mitigation and energy-efficient VOC control. This gap hinders the development of responsive, healthy, and sustainable indoor environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a machine learning model, trained on historical indoor air quality and operational data, can accurately predict optimal, real-time control parameters (e.g., airflow rates, photocatalytic system intensity) for novel pollutant mixtures to simultaneously minimize infection risk and VOC concentrations.
proposals.method: We will develop a hybrid predictive framework. First, a Gradient Boosting Regressor (e.g., XGBoost) will be trained on multi-source data (sensor readings for VOCs/CO2, occupancy, outdoor conditions, system operations) to predict future pollutant concentrations. Second, a Reinforcement Learning (RL) agent, using a Deep Q-Network (DQN) architecture, will be trained to recommend control actions by optimizing a reward function that weights infection risk (using a Wells-Riley model proxy) and VOC exposure against energy use. The system will be validated in a simulated environment built on CONTAM/EnergyPlus.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, data-driven control framework that moves beyond static setpoints or single-pollutant strategies. It will provide a methodology for the intelligent, predictive operation of hybrid ventilation and photocatalytic systems tailored to complex, real-world pollutant mixtures. The outcome is a significant step towards autonomous building systems that dynamically prioritize health risks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of an ML-based multi-pollutant predictor with an RL-based controller explicitly designed to jointly optimize two distinct health risk metrics (infection and VOC exposure) under energy constraints, a synthesis not yet fully explored in pre-2026 building control literature.

proposals.title: A Probabilistic Trajectory Forecasting and Model Predictive Control Framework for Proactive Indoor Air Quality and Infection Risk Management
proposals.motivation: Dynamic indoor environments with moving occupants and transient pollutant sources (e.g., VOCs, bioaerosols) challenge conventional reactive HVAC control, leading to energy waste or compromised air quality. The COVID-19 pandemic has underscored the need for real-time infection risk mitigation, yet purely reactive systems cannot preemptively adjust to occupant movement. A predictive, closed-loop approach is required to simultaneously ensure health and energy efficiency in public buildings.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating probabilistic forecasting of occupant trajectories and source dynamics into a Model Predictive Control (MPC) framework will yield a more robust and energy-efficient control strategy for ventilation and air cleaning systems than state-of-the-art reactive or schedule-based methods.
proposals.method: The system will fuse real-time IoT sensor data (CO2, PM, VOCs, occupancy counts) with building information models. A probabilistic forecasting module, using techniques like Gaussian Processes or Conditional Random Fields, will predict short-term occupant and source location distributions based on historical patterns and contextual data (e.g., time of day). These forecasted probability maps will serve as dynamic inputs to a multi-objective MPC optimizer, which will compute optimal setpoints for ventilation rates and photocatalytic oxidation (PCO) system operation to minimize predicted infection risk (using Wells-Riley or dose-response models) and VOC concentrations while constraining energy use.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, integrated control architecture that explicitly accounts for future uncertainty in human dynamics. It will provide a validated simulation framework and control logic that demonstrably reduces energy consumption while maintaining stringent indoor air quality and infection risk targets under dynamic conditions, advancing the field of intelligent building operation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the closed-loop integration of probabilistic human trajectory forecasting—a concept from robotics and computer vision—with physics-based MPC for indoor environmental control, creating a proactive system rather than a reactive one, which is a significant departure from current HVAC and air cleaning control paradigms.

proposals.title: A Dynamic Agent-Based Model for Assessing Infection Risk and VOC Exposure from Moving Occupants and Intermittent Sources in Office Environments
proposals.motivation: Current indoor air quality (IAQ) and infection risk models predominantly assume static occupant positions and constant source locations, which oversimplifies real-world office dynamics. This limitation is critical for accurately assessing exposure to airborne pathogens (e.g., SARS-CoV-2) and volatile organic compounds (VOCs) from intermittent sources like printers or cleaning products, as movement alters airflow patterns and personal exposure. A dynamic modeling framework is needed to inform the intelligent, demand-controlled operation of ventilation and air cleaning systems for simultaneous energy efficiency and health protection.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating dynamic, agent-based occupant movement and intermittent source activity into coupled computational fluid dynamics (CFD) and multi-zone models will yield significantly different, and more accurate, spatiotemporal predictions of personal infection risk and VOC exposure compared to static-assumption models.
proposals.method: The research will develop a hybrid modeling architecture. An agent-based model (ABM), using pre-defined or data-driven activity schedules, will simulate occupant movement and intermittent source operation (e.g., printer use). This ABM will dynamically interface with a transient CFD model (for high-resolution local airflow) and a multi-zone model (for whole-building ventilation analysis). The Wells-Riley model will be adapted for dynamic infection risk calculation per agent, while a VOC mass balance model will track concentrations from moving sources. The system will be validated against controlled chamber data with simulated movement and tracer gas sources.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel simulation tool for quantifying the impact of human movement on IAQ and disease transmission, directly supporting the design of next-generation intelligent building control systems. It will deliver new insights into peak personal exposure events and inform targeted ventilation strategies that adapt to real-time occupancy patterns, bridging the gap between energy-focused and health-focused building operation.
proposals.novelty: While agent-based models exist for pedestrian dynamics, their tight integration with transient CFD for personalized, multi-physics IAQ and infection risk assessment in offices is novel. This moves beyond evaluating 'average' room conditions to model real-time, individual exposure based on specific movement paths and proximity to dynamic sources.

proposals.title: A Network-Based Adaptive Ventilation Control System for Mitigating Infection Risk and VOC Exposure in Barrier-Rich Indoor Environments
proposals.motivation: Conventional demand-controlled ventilation (DCV) often treats zones independently, failing to account for dynamic airflow disruptions caused by physical barriers (e.g., partitions, furniture) which can create localized pockets of poor air quality and elevated infection risk. This is critical as pre-2026 research emphasizes the need for energy-efficient systems that also actively mitigate airborne pathogen transmission and VOC accumulation. A holistic, system-level control strategy that responds to real-time airflow 'traffic' is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: By modeling a ventilation network with barriers as dynamic constraints and applying adaptive traffic flow control algorithms, a centralized controller can dynamically redistribute supply airflow to minimize local contaminant concentration (e.g., CO2, VOCs, surrogate aerosols) and energy use more effectively than zone-independent DCV.
proposals.method: The research will develop a sensor network (CO2, total VOCs, differential pressure) to monitor zones and barrier junctions. A graph-based model of the airflow network will be created, where nodes represent zones/vents and edges represent airflow paths with barrier-induced resistance. A Model Predictive Control (MPC) framework, analogous to adaptive traffic signal control, will be implemented. The MPC optimizer will use real-time sensor data and short-term predictions of occupancy/VOC generation to solve for optimal, dynamic supply air rates at each vent, balancing energy use against a multi-pollutant risk index derived from concentration thresholds.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, system-level control framework for ventilation that explicitly manages barrier-induced airflow disruptions. It will provide a methodology to quantify and mitigate localized infection risk and VOC exposure through dynamic airflow redistribution, advancing beyond standard CO2-based DCV. The results will offer a blueprint for integrating infection risk metrics directly into real-time HVAC operational logic.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct translation of network-level adaptive traffic flow control principles to ventilation system operation, treating barriers as dynamic, manageable constraints rather than static design flaws. This system-level, constraint-aware optimization represents a significant shift from conventional zone-based or occupancy-only DCV approaches prevalent pre-2026.

proposals.title: A Sequential Multi-Fidelity Validation Framework for Robust Prediction of Indoor Airflow and Infection Risk Under Dynamic Operational Conditions
proposals.motivation: Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) and multi-zone models are essential for designing ventilation systems that mitigate airborne infection risk and control VOC exposure. However, model validation is often limited to single, steady-state scenarios, failing to capture performance under the dynamic, varied conditions of real buildings. This lack of robust validation protocols undermines confidence in simulation-based decisions for energy-efficient and healthy building operation.
proposals.hypothesis: A sequential validation protocol, which first calibrates fundamental turbulence and species transport parameters against high-fidelity chamber data and then rigorously tests the model's predictive power across a designed matrix of real-world operational perturbations, will yield a simulation tool with significantly improved generalizability and reliability for infection risk and contaminant concentration assessment.
proposals.method: The protocol begins with high-resolution Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV) and tracer gas (e.g., SF6) measurements in a controlled environmental chamber to calibrate a CFD model's turbulence (e.g., RANS k-ε) and species transport parameters. This calibrated model is then used to simulate a series of tracer gas decay tests in an instrumented office space, following a designed experimental matrix that systematically varies HVAC supply rates, occupancy heat loads, and window opening fractions. Model performance is quantitatively evaluated across the entire domain using metrics like the Normalized Mean Square Error (NMSE) and Fraction of Predictions within a Factor of Two (FAC2).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a structured, transparent, and rigorous validation methodology for the indoor airflow modeling community, moving beyond single-point calibration. It will deliver a benchmarked simulation tool and dataset for predicting contaminant transport under dynamic conditions, directly supporting the design of intelligent building control systems that balance infection risk mitigation and energy efficiency.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit, sequential multi-fidelity structure and the adversarial testing of the model against a designed matrix of real-world operational changes, ensuring robustness rather than mere calibration to a single ideal state, which is a critical gap in pre-2026 practice.

proposals.title: A Model Predictive Control Framework for Coordinated Airborne Infection Risk Mitigation: Integrating Physical Barriers, Air Cleaning, and Occupancy in Public Buildings
proposals.motivation: Post-pandemic, managing airborne infection risk in public buildings remains critical, yet current approaches often deploy interventions like air cleaners and physical barriers statically, leading to energy inefficiency and suboptimal protection. There is a recognized need for intelligent building systems that can dynamically coordinate multiple control strategies based on real-time conditions to maintain target risk levels while minimizing operational costs, a gap identified in recent work on infection risk mitigation and energy efficiency.
proposals.hypothesis: A unified, sensor-driven control system that dynamically coordinates physical barriers, portable air cleaner operation, and occupancy density can maintain a specified airborne infection risk threshold with significantly lower energy consumption compared to static or independently operating interventions.
proposals.method: The research will develop a Model Predictive Control (MPC) framework integrating real-time inputs from CO2, PM2.5 sensors, and occupancy counters. A modified Wells-Riley model will serve as the core risk predictor. The MPC optimizer, using a cost function weighting energy use (from air cleaner power) and risk deviation, will generate control signals to adjust air cleaner fan speeds and recommend optimal occupancy schedules. The system will be prototyped and validated in a controlled environmental chamber simulating a classroom or office, with scenarios testing response to varying occupancy and simulated exhalation events.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated control architecture for the synergistic, energy-aware management of multi-layered indoor health interventions. It will deliver a quantitative framework for trading off infection risk against energy cost in real-time, advancing the field of intelligent building operation beyond single-objective ventilation control or static air cleaning.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the dynamic, closed-loop coordination of heterogeneous risk controls (physical barriers, air cleaners, occupancy) within a single optimization framework, moving beyond the current paradigm of reviewing individual technologies or using sensors for only one control modality (e.g., ventilation).

proposals.title: A Dynamic Control Framework for Synergistic Low-Cost Interventions to Protect Peripheral Workstations from Airborne Contaminants
proposals.motivation: In open-plan offices, workstations far from HVAC supply diffusers experience poor air mixing and higher exposure to airborne infectious agents and VOCs, posing a significant health risk. Retrofitting centralized systems for these 'hard-to-reach' zones is often prohibitively expensive and energy-intensive. There is a critical need for practical, low-cost localized protection strategies that integrate multiple interventions effectively.
proposals.hypothesis: A dynamically controlled system that synchronizes adjustable desk barriers, portable air cleaners with HEPA/activated carbon filters, and occupancy-aware ventilation can create effective localized protection zones in peripheral areas, maintaining target air quality (infection risk and VOC concentration) at a fraction of the energy cost of full-building system upgrades.
proposals.method: The research will employ a three-phase approach: 1) Experimental characterization in a climate chamber mock-up of a peripheral zone, measuring contaminant (tracer gas and surrogate particles) dispersion under different barrier configurations, cleaner placements (using CADR metrics), and background ventilation rates. 2) Development of a dynamic control algorithm using a rule-based system informed by real-time feedback from low-cost PM2.5, CO2, and total VOC sensors. The algorithm will adjust portable cleaner fan speed and recommend barrier repositioning (e.g., via a user interface) based on occupancy schedules and contaminant concentration thresholds. 3) Validation through simulation using CONTAM and infection risk models (e.g., Wells-Riley) to quantify the relative effectiveness and energy use compared to static interventions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, practical framework for optimizing the synergy of low-cost, non-invasive interventions for indoor air quality management in existing buildings. It will deliver specific design and control guidelines for barrier geometry, cleaner placement, and operational logic to mitigate exposure risks in poorly ventilated zones, contributing to energy-efficient retrofits.
proposals.novelty: While individual elements (barriers, cleaners) have been studied in isolation, the novelty lies in their integrated, dynamic control based on real-time sensing and occupancy to create an adaptive protection system specifically for architecturally disadvantaged locations, moving beyond static, standalone solutions.

It uses data streams from IoT sensors and building information to estimate current occupant and source locations while predicting their likely short-term future trajectories based on historical behavior patterns and contextual cues. The forecasted probabilistic states are then fed into a model predictive control (MPC) framework to optimize environmental system setpoints proactively. This results in a robust, computationally feasible solution that maintains performance and energy efficiency despite dynamic conditions.

ideas.description: This research investigates the integrated design and real-time operation of combined interventions—physical barriers, portable air cleaners, and occupant scheduling—specifically for workstations located beyond 4m from primary HVAC outlets. The core novelty lies in developing and testing dynamic control strategies that synchronize these low-cost elements based on occupancy and real-time air quality feedback to create effective, localized protection zones where centralized ventilation is weak. The outcome will be a practical framework for optimizing the synergy between adjustable barrier designs, cleaner placement/operation, and scheduling to maintain air quality targets in hard-to-reach areas with minimal energy and infrastructure cost.

ideas.title: Adaptive Traffic Flow Control for Ventilation Systems
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of a constant ventilation supply air rate by drawing an analogy from adaptive traffic flow control in transportation engineering. The analogous solution uses real-time sensor data to dynamically adjust traffic signal timing in response to actual demand and disruptions, rather than relying on fixed schedules. Adapted to building ventilation, this suggests a research idea to model and implement a demand-controlled ventilation system that interacts intelligently with barrier-induced airflow. The system would use a network of sensors to monitor local air quality and pressure conditions, particularly around physical barriers that disrupt intended airflow patterns. A central controller would then dynamically adjust supply air rates to different zones or individual vents, optimizing for energy efficiency and air quality by responding to the real-time 'traffic' of air, treating barriers as dynamic constraints to be managed rather than static design flaws.

ideas.title: Geomechanics-Aware Model Predictive Control for Risk-Optimized CAES Operation
ideas.description: This research synthesizes geomechanical integrity monitoring from carbon capture and storage (CCS) with advanced process control from chemical engineering to develop a novel, risk-aware operational framework for Compressed Air Energy Storage (CAES). The core innovation is a Model Predictive Control (MPC) algorithm whose internal dynamic model and constraints are continuously updated in real-time by a geomechanical observability module, fed by data from techniques like distributed acoustic sensing and passive seismic monitoring. This allows the controller to predictively adjust charge/discharge rates and pressure trajectories to minimize the risk of cavern failure or fatigue, while still meeting grid flexibility targets. The result is a CAES system that can safely push its operational envelopes, maximizing economic value and renewable integration without compromising long-term geological asset integrity.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Modeling to Forecast CAES Site Suitability and Cumulative Impact
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from conservation biology, where Species Distribution Models (SDMs) forecast habitat suitability and cumulative impacts on ecosystems, to the problem of assessing the environmental impact of large-scale Compressed Air Energy Storage (CAES) deployment. The analogous solution involves creating predictive models based on key environmental variables. Adapted to CAES, the research would develop a Geotechnical and Environmental Suitability Model (GESM) that integrates variables such as geological formation integrity, aquifer characteristics, seismic risk, surface land use, and existing infrastructure. This model would conduct a proactive, spatially-explicit lifecycle analysis to forecast the geological impacts and cumulative working fluid emission risks of potential CAES sites at scale, guiding sustainable deployment and pre-empting regional environmental conflicts.

ideas.title: Apply Pharmacokinetic Model Predictive Control to CAES for Grid Stability
ideas.description: This idea adapts the model predictive control (MPC) framework from pharmacokinetic drug delivery systems to the operation of Compressed Air Energy Storage (CAES). In pharmacology, MPC uses a patient-specific pharmacokinetic-pharmacodynamic model to predict the effect of a drug infusion and optimally adjust the rate to maintain a target physiological state despite metabolic disturbances. The analogous solution is adapted by treating the CAES system as the 'patient,' with its complex thermodynamic and fluid-dynamic responses modeled as the 'PK/PD' system. The grid imbalance or renewable forecast error acts as the metabolic disturbance. The adapted research idea is to implement an advanced MPC that uses this high-fidelity CAES model as its internal predictive plant. The controller will optimize the charge/discharge profile not just for energy arbitrage, but primarily for real-time grid stability services (e.g., frequency regulation, ramp rate control), dynamically managing the CAES system's inherent lags and energy losses to provide precise, forecast-informed power injection or absorption.

ideas.title: Geology-Constrained Inverse CFD Modeling for CAES Cavern Digital Twins
ideas.description: This research develops a closed-loop system that combines high-fidelity aerospace-grade CFD with geomechanical modeling and Bayesian inversion. Instead of a purely forward simulation, the framework uses historical and real-time operational data from a compressed air energy storage (CAES) facility to inversely solve for the cavern's unknown geological properties, such as effective permeability and wall heat transfer coefficients. The CFD model is then continuously updated within an uncertainty quantification framework, creating a probabilistically validated digital twin that predicts flow dynamics and thermal behavior specific to the actual cavern. This approach ensures predictive power is grounded in observable plant performance, transforming CFD from a speculative design tool into a robust operational and planning asset.

ideas.title: Decouple CAES from the grid: Direct pneumatic integration for industrial symbiosis
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that energy storage must be electrical by applying the principle of industrial symbiosis from the field of industrial ecology. The analogous solution involves using waste outputs (like heat or steam) directly as inputs for neighboring processes, avoiding conversion losses. Adapted to CAES, this means decoupling the storage system from the electrical grid and integrating it directly into industrial processes that require compressed air or mechanical work. By co-locating CAES with industrial air users or connecting them via dedicated pipelines, the stored energy can be delivered as pneumatic power, creating a more efficient 'industrial symbiosis' where the storage medium is the final product, bypassing the inefficient air-to-electricity conversion step entirely.

ideas.title: Apply Ecological Network Analysis (ENA) to CAES Exergy Flows
ideas.description: This research adapts the methodology of Ecological Network Analysis (ENA) from Systems Ecology to perform a novel, system-level exergy analysis of Compressed Air Energy Storage (CAES). In ecology, ENA maps energy and material flows through food webs to quantify system structure, efficiency, and resilience. Analogously, we map all exergy flows (compression, storage, expansion, heat exchange) through a CAES system's components, treating them as a directed network. By applying ENA metrics—such as the cycling index (to quantify beneficial exergy recirculation vs. wasteful dissipation), ascendency (to measure the organization and efficiency of the exergy flow structure), and control analysis (to identify which components exert the greatest influence on overall exergy destruction)—we can pinpoint systemic, rather than just local, inefficiencies. This network-based exergy analysis reveals hidden interdependencies and structural bottlenecks, guiding holistic design and control strategies to minimize total exergy loss and maximize the available work output of the full CAES cycle.

ideas.title: Modeling Multi-Sensory Distraction Thresholds for Hybrid Office Configurations
ideas.description: This research moves beyond simply testing physical partitions to model the integrated effects of auditory, visual, and spatial factors on workplace effectiveness. Using a controlled experimental lab simulating an office environment, it employs biometric sensors, performance tasks, and subjective surveys to measure cognitive load and privacy perception under varied conditions. The goal is to generate a predictive model that identifies optimal, cost-effective combinations of architectural elements (e.g., partition height), ambient controls (e.g., soundscape), and policy (e.g., activity zoning) to mitigate distraction. This provides evidence-based design principles for creating hybrid workspaces that support focused work without resorting to fully enclosed, inflexible layouts.

ideas.title: Techno-Exergetic Co-Optimization for CAES Design
ideas.description: This research integrates second-law (exergy) analysis with multi-objective techno-economic optimization to create a unified design framework for Compressed Air Energy Storage (CAES) systems. The approach formulates a constrained optimization problem where exergy destruction from all components (compressors, expanders, thermal stores, caverns) is calculated dynamically and minimized alongside the levelized cost of storage (LCOS). This forces a direct trade-off analysis between thermodynamic perfection and economic practicality, generating Pareto-optimal design frontiers. The outcome is a decision-support tool that identifies specific, cost-effective design modifications—such as optimal staging, heat integration, and component sizing—that yield the greatest improvement in overall system value, not just efficiency.

ideas.title: Hybrid CAES with Phase-Change Material Buffers for Multi-Timescale Dispatch
ideas.description: This idea proposes a hybrid energy storage system inspired by the memory hierarchy in computer architecture. Just as computers use cache, RAM, and disk storage in tandem for optimal performance, this system integrates CAES with batteries and ultra-fast storage (e.g., flywheels). The analogous solution of tiered, managed data movement is adapted into an intelligent power management controller that treats each storage technology as a tier in an energy 'memory' hierarchy. It dispatches power from the fastest (but smallest capacity) tier for immediate grid services, uses batteries for short-duration balancing, and reserves the large-capacity CAES for long-duration bulk energy shifting. This combines the strengths of each technology—speed, power density, and energy density—into a single, highly dispatchable asset that improves overall system performance, reliability, and cost-effectiveness.

ideas.title: Inverse problem: design VOC mixture for testing
ideas.description: Determine the most challenging representative VOC mixture to stress-test and benchmark PCO air purifiers.

ideas.title: A Hybrid Real-Time Integrity Dashboard for CAES by Fusing High-Frequency Operational Monitoring with CCS-Style Baseline Analysis
ideas.description: This research designs a cavern integrity monitoring system specifically for the rapid pressure cycles of Compressed Air Energy Storage (CAES). It combines high-temporal-resolution tools like distributed fiber optic sensing (for strain and temperature) and microseismic arrays, tuned for fatigue and rapid deformation detection, with the long-term baseline geochemical and geomechanical analysis techniques from Carbon Capture and Storage (CCS) projects. The integrated framework provides a real-time dashboard that distinguishes between benign operational strains and precursor signals of significant integrity loss, enabling predictive maintenance. This approach directly addresses the critical mismatch in timescale and risk profile between the slow seepage concerns of CCS and the fast cyclic fatigue of CAES.

ideas.title: Experimental Validation of Hybrid CAES System Dynamics Using Tracer Gas Techniques
ideas.description: This research direction develops and deploys experimental tracer gas techniques to validate high-fidelity simulations of hybrid Compressed Air Energy Storage (CAES) systems integrated with thermal or battery storage. By injecting tracer gases or particles into a controlled, lab-scale or prototype hybrid CAES apparatus, researchers can directly measure air mixing, heat transfer between storage media, and transient response during charge/discharge cycles. This provides unprecedented empirical data to calibrate and verify multi-physics models of the coupled systems. The validated models can then be used to precisely optimize the synergistic operation of the combined storage technologies, improving round-trip efficiency and system longevity beyond what is possible with separate simulation or component-level testing.

ideas.title: Modeling Barrier-Ventilation Interaction Using River Hydraulics and Scour Protection Analogies
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from river hydraulics and scour protection engineering. In that field, the effectiveness of in-stream structures is evaluated by modeling how different upstream flow conditions interact with the structure's geometry to create specific downstream flow patterns, vortices, and scour zones. Adapting this solution, the research will treat indoor ventilation as a fluid dynamics problem. It will employ methods like computational fluid dynamics (CFD) and physical analog modeling (e.g., water tables for flow visualization) to systematically test how various air supply patterns (the upstream 'flow' conditions) interact with different physical barrier designs. The outcome will be a detailed mapping of the resulting air currents, vortex formation, and zones of high vs. low aerosol concentration around barriers, providing a physics-based metric for barrier effectiveness under different ventilation modes.

ideas.title: Modular, Multi-Vector CAES Hubs for Industrial Clusters and Grid Services
ideas.description: This redesigned system overcomes the efficiency and economic limitations of dedicated industrial CAES by creating a modular, transportable storage hub. It employs containerized compression/expansion units and storage that can be deployed at industrial parks or connected to existing gas infrastructure, allowing it to serve multiple nearby facilities. The hub's control system dynamically allocates stored energy to either direct compressed air for industrial processes, electricity generation for the grid, or ancillary services, maximizing utilization and revenue. This multi-vector approach improves the business case by aggregating demand and accessing higher-value grid markets, making the storage asset financially viable despite thermodynamic losses.

ideas.title: High-Fidelity CFD-Driven Design of Micro-CAES Systems for Distributed Energy and Mobility
ideas.description: This research applies high-fidelity Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD), typically used for aerospace systems and large-scale CAES caverns, to the micro-scale design of safe, efficient compressed air energy storage units for residential and EV applications. The core innovation is using these advanced simulations to model transient, non-ideal gas behavior, intricate heat transfer, and fluid-structure interactions within small, engineered pressure vessels or novel compact geometries. This CFD-driven approach allows for the optimization of internal baffles, phase-change material integration, and charge/discharge port design to maximize energy density and cycle efficiency at small scales. The outcome is a new design paradigm for micro-CAES, moving from empirical scaling to first-principles virtual prototyping, enabling safe, compact systems suitable for home energy management or vehicle integration.

ideas.title: Apply industrial sensor network analytics to classrooms

ideas.title: Adapting Pharmaceutical QSAR to CAES Materials Discovery
ideas.description: This idea applies the core methodology from pharmaceutical Quantitative Structure-Activity Relationship (QSAR) modeling to the problem of discovering materials for Compressed Air Energy Storage (CAES) components. In drug discovery, QSAR correlates molecular descriptors with biological activity to enable virtual screening. The adapted solution is to develop Quantitative Structure-Property Relationship (QSPR) models within a materials informatics framework. Material descriptors (e.g., from high-throughput calculations or existing databases) will be used to train machine learning models to predict critical CAES performance properties like thermal stability, mechanical strength under pressure cycling, and corrosion resistance. This data-driven approach allows for the rapid in-silico identification and optimization of novel or composite materials specifically tailored for heat exchangers and containment liners, significantly accelerating the design cycle compared to traditional trial-and-error experimentation.

ideas.title: Pathogen Inactivation via Engineered Thermal Plumes from CAES Waste Heat
ideas.description: This research investigates the use of waste heat from Compressed Air Energy Storage (CAES) systems to generate engineered thermal plumes, creating controlled, large-scale atmospheric conditions. The primary objective is to study how these specific heat and airflow dynamics affect the dispersion, stability, and inactivation of diverse airborne pathogens, categorized by their aerosolized particle sizes. This approach transforms district heating infrastructure into a living laboratory for environmental virology and aerosol science, enabling field studies at a community-relevant scale. The findings could lead to dual-purpose CAES designs that provide both thermal energy and a potential means of reducing ambient pathogen loads in adjacent areas.

ideas.title: Modular, Multi-Fluid Energy Storage Pods for Distributed Energy Systems
ideas.description: This research develops a standardized, small-scale pressurized storage module ('pod') designed for safe integration at residential, commercial, or EV charging hub levels. Each pod is engineered to be fluid-agnostic, capable of storing either compressed air for short-duration power or hydrogen for longer-duration storage and fuel applications, with integrated systems for safe conversion and management. The core innovation is a smart, modular architecture that allows these pods to be networked, creating a distributed, dual-function storage asset that can collectively respond to both local energy management needs and wider grid signals for flexibility. This enables a single physical asset to maximize its utility and economic value by adapting its stored medium to match seasonal, diurnal, or market conditions.

ideas.title: Modular, High-Pressure Micro-Vessel Arrays for Residential CAES
ideas.description: This research adapts a safety and scaling solution from biomedical engineering to the problem of small-scale Compressed Air Energy Storage (CAES). In portable medical devices, high-pressure gases are stored not in one large tank but in arrays of many small, high-strength composite micro-vessels, massively reducing the risk associated with a single pressure vessel failure. We propose to design a residential or EV-integrated CAES system using this principle. The core would be a modular bank of hundreds of small, commercially available carbon-fiber pressure vessels, each with a very low individual energy capacity. They would be connected via an intelligent manifold that controls charging/discharging airflow. This approach inherently enhances safety for home/vehicle use, allows the storage system to be shaped to fit available spaces (walls, under-floor, vehicle sub-frame), and utilizes well-understood, certified small pressure vessel technology, potentially accelerating development and regulatory approval compared to designing a novel large-scale residential pressure vessel.

ideas.title: Dynamic, Grid-Coupled Modeling of Spatially Explicit Environmental Impacts from Scaling CAES
ideas.description: This research develops a high-resolution, spatio-temporal modeling framework to assess the non-linear environmental impacts of scaling Compressed Air Energy Storage (CAES). It integrates a geotechnical-geochemical subsurface simulator with a forward-looking grid dispatch model to project how CAES deployment, across specific geological formations, interacts with future renewable energy pathways. The system evaluates dynamic feedbacks, such as how localized geological impacts and working fluid emissions shift with varying CAES utilization and competing storage technologies, providing a robust tool for proactive, site-specific environmental and grid planning.

ideas.title: Geologically-Active Smart Liners: Materials Informatics for CAES Caverns that Monitor and Stabilize Geology
ideas.description: This research direction applies data-driven materials discovery to design and fabricate 'smart' composite liners for Compressed Air Energy Storage (CAES) caverns. These liners integrate embedded sensors for strain, pressure, and geochemical activity, manufactured from materials optimized for durability and sensing capability. The continuous data stream from the liner enables a precise, feedback-controlled inverse approach to pressure management, allowing the CAES operation to actively counteract subsidence or seismic stress in real-time. Thus, the cavern becomes an intelligent geotechnical actuator, where the material of the liner itself is key to both storing energy and stabilizing the surrounding geology.

ideas.title: Dynamic Acoustic Material Optimization for Adaptive Occupant-Centric Soundscapes
ideas.description: This research fuses dynamic acoustic modeling with materials informatics to develop a system for adaptive, occupant-centric acoustic environments. It uses real-time tracking of moving occupants and noise sources to predict the evolving soundscape within a space. A materials informatics engine then identifies or designs non-static surface materials or modular components (e.g., with tunable porosity, stiffness, or resonant frequency) whose optimal acoustic properties change based on the predicted sound field. The synergy enables the proactive or reactive adjustment of localized acoustic conditions—such as targeted absorption or diffusion—to maintain desired acoustic performance despite occupant and source movement, moving beyond static room design.

ideas.title: Curtailment CAES with Integrated Industrial Waste Heat Valorization
ideas.description: This design repurposes Compressed Air Energy Storage (CAES) for curtailment absorption but directly couples it to a constant, high-temperature industrial process. The massive compression heat, normally a loss in low-efficiency CAES, becomes the primary thermal input for applications like cement calcination or steam generation. The system economically absorbs excess renewable generation by selling both dispatched electricity and thermal commodity output, making the low round-trip efficiency financially sustainable. The value of curtailed energy is thus preserved in two distinct revenue streams rather than being largely wasted.

ideas.title: Performance-Linked Infrastructure Model for CAES: Blending Regulatory Certainty with Efficiency Incentives
ideas.description: This research designs a hybrid regulatory-financial model for Compressed Air Energy Storage (CAES) that moves beyond the binary choice of pure market participation or cost-based public utility. The core system is a Regulated Asset Base (RAB) framework, ensuring investment recovery for the capital-intensive infrastructure through regulated grid charges. Crucially, the operator's total allowed revenue is dynamically adjusted via a performance scorecard, linking a substantial portion of income to metrics like operational efficiency, reliability during system stress, and technology improvement rates. This model aims to secure the long-term, location-specific deployment needed for grid resilience while directly incentivizing cost discipline and innovation, preventing the inefficiencies of a pure cost-of-service monopoly.

ideas.title: Biological Detritus Accumulation for Curtailment Absorption
ideas.description: This idea inverts the need for high round-trip efficiency in Compressed Air Energy Storage (CAES) by drawing an analogy from ecology and nutrient cycling. In ecosystems, decomposers specialize in absorbing massive, low-quality inputs of detritus; their efficiency in converting it is secondary to their capacity to prevent system overload. The analogous solution is to design CAES primarily as a 'grid decomposer' for curtailment absorption. Adapted to the research context, this means valuing massive storage capacity over efficiency. The CAES system would be optimized for minimal capital cost per unit of storage volume and maximal intake power, with the core function being to absorb excess renewable generation that would otherwise be wasted. The key performance indicator shifts from round-trip efficiency to 'curtailment absorption capacity per dollar,' accepting low conversion efficiency as the necessary trade-off for providing a cheap, large-scale buffer that stabilizes the grid by monetizing otherwise valueless surplus energy.

ideas.title: Dynamic Ventilation Response to Spatially-Variant, Transient Exhalation Profiles in Barrier-Modulated Airflows
ideas.description: This research fuses demand-controlled ventilation (DCV) interacting with barriers and the non-uniform nature of human exhalation to create a novel, high-resolution control paradigm. It investigates how real-time sensing of transient, high-intensity exhalation events (like coughing or loud speaking) from specific locations should dynamically modulate local and global ventilation rates, especially when barriers alter expected airflow paths and pollutant dispersion. The core novelty is a control system that targets and mitigates acute, localized airborne transmission risks by integrating spatiotemporal exhalation data with computational fluid dynamics (CFD) models of the barrier-modified environment, moving beyond steady-state, uniform occupant assumptions.

ideas.title: A Federated, Multi-Site CAES Network with Coordinated Dispatch Protocol
ideas.description: This research adapts the architecture and routing logic of large-scale Content Delivery Networks (CDNs) from distributed computing to design a continental-scale Compressed Air Energy Storage (CAES) backbone. The analogous CDN solution involves a geographically distributed network of servers with intelligent protocols for optimal content routing and load balancing. Adapted to the energy context, the idea is to design a federated, multi-site CAES network where multiple, geographically dispersed geological storage facilities are integrated not just physically, but through a coordinated digital dispatch protocol. This 'Energy Delivery Network' protocol would dynamically manage energy flows—directing surplus renewable generation to the most optimal storage site and dispatching stored energy from the best location to meet demand—based on a real-time calculus of grid conditions, storage site status, and transmission constraints. The goal is to create a unified, continent-scale energy shifting backbone that appears as a single, highly flexible resource to grid operators, dramatically improving the utilization of intermittent renewables and grid stability.
